- Truth About Living in Texas No One Talks About: Kind: captions Language: en Texas! The Lone Star State, with booming cities,&nbsp; a huge influx of new residents, and great BBQ. A&nbsp;&nbsp; place where everything is bigger and better but&nbsp; still surprisingly cheap…with almost no taxes!&nbsp; Well, that’s the reputation. But&nbsp; the reality is way more complicated.&nbsp; In fact, a 2023 study by CNBC even found that&nbsp; Texas was the worst state in America to live in,&nbsp;&nbsp; just ahead of the sun-scorched desert of&nbsp; Arizona. How can that be? Let’s take a closer&nbsp;&nbsp; look at the truth about Texas no one talks about. We’ll start with some basics: the weather. While&nbsp;&nbsp; you may think the weather in Texas is milder than&nbsp; in many states - particularly if you currently&nbsp;&nbsp; live in the frigid north of the country - it can&nbsp; also be very extreme. The state sits right in the&nbsp;&nbsp; middle of an area known as “Tornado Alley”, which&nbsp; is…well, exactly what it sounds like. Basically,&nbsp;&nbsp; a whole lot of tornadoes pass through&nbsp; it, destroying everything in their wake.&nbsp; In fact, Texas is ranked in the top five&nbsp; states nationwide for most tornadoes,&nbsp;&nbsp; averaging around 120 per year, or in&nbsp; other words, one tornado every three days.&nbsp;&nbsp; North Texas residents, especially, need to&nbsp; have access to an underground shelter nearby,&nbsp;&nbsp; since they get hit with more tornadoes than most. While you can mostly protect yourself from&nbsp;&nbsp; tornadoes by hiding underground, you can’t take&nbsp; your whole house with you. And 250 mph (402 kph)&nbsp;&nbsp; winds don’t mix well with roofs and walls.&nbsp; That means that unless you have really great,&nbsp;&nbsp; usually extra and specific, tornado insurance –&nbsp; unlikely as you’ll see later on – tornadoes can&nbsp;&nbsp; deal some devastating damage to your finances. If you’re in the southeast, you don’t need to&nbsp;&nbsp; worry that much about tornadoes…but you will&nbsp; get slammed by hurricanes. There’s almost&nbsp;&nbsp; no corner of the state that escapes natural&nbsp; disasters. Since Texas is right on the Gulf&nbsp;&nbsp; of America - formerly the Gulf Of Mexico - that&nbsp; means the coast, and even some inland cities,&nbsp;&nbsp; are especially vulnerable. Since some of&nbsp; Texas’ biggest cities - including Houston,&nbsp;&nbsp; the fourth biggest city in the country - sit&nbsp; right on the coast, that creates a huge problem.&nbsp; On August 25, 2017, Hurricane Harvey made&nbsp; landfall just northeast of Corpus Christi,&nbsp;&nbsp; having intensified into a Category 4 hurricane&nbsp; while barreling towards the Texas coast. Wind&nbsp;&nbsp; gusts took out the International Airport,&nbsp; and caused severe damage to coastal towns,&nbsp;&nbsp; destroying hundreds of homes and&nbsp; businesses in the Rockport and Fulton area.&nbsp; Water and power were cut off for weeks - something&nbsp; you’ll soon see isn’t all that rare for Texas. 103&nbsp;&nbsp; people died in the state, and the National Oceanic&nbsp; and Atmospheric Administration - NOAA - estimated&nbsp;&nbsp; the total damage to be around $125 billion,&nbsp; though some numbers are even higher.&nbsp; Unfortunately, hurricane season will likely get&nbsp; worse and worse over time due to climate change,&nbsp;&nbsp; making living on the Texas coast pretty risky. And if all that wasn’t enough, thanks to dry,&nbsp;&nbsp; hot conditions in large sections of the state,&nbsp; Texas is often scorched by wildfires. In February&nbsp;&nbsp; 2024, the Smokehouse Creek Fire roared into&nbsp; existence after a utility pole downed a power&nbsp;&nbsp; line. It rapidly covered over 1.1 million&nbsp; acres (445,154 hectares) in just a week,&nbsp;&nbsp; burning an area slightly bigger than Rhode&nbsp; Island. The damage was so intense that it&nbsp;&nbsp; ended up being the biggest fire in Texas history,&nbsp; and the ninth biggest fire ever in U.S. history.&nbsp; And we haven’t even touched the golf-ball-sized&nbsp; hail, flash floods, and thunderstorms.&nbsp; This cornucopia of disasters has, naturally,&nbsp; started affecting homeowners’ ability to get&nbsp;&nbsp; insured. The risk from extreme weather&nbsp; is increasing due to climate change,&nbsp;&nbsp; and many insurance companies are imposing insane&nbsp; premiums in Houston and the surrounding coast,&nbsp;&nbsp; or even refusing to insure homeowners at all. According to Bankrate, Texas is currently the&nbsp;&nbsp; sixth most expensive state for home insurance,&nbsp; with an average annual premium of just over&nbsp;&nbsp; $4,000 for a $300,000 home. Insurance&nbsp; website Insurify, believes that,&nbsp;&nbsp; due to increasingly severe weather, Texas&nbsp; will climb to fifth place by the end of 2025.&nbsp; So how much is the weather really&nbsp; worsening in the Lone Star State?&nbsp; Well, NOAA has been tracking the severity of&nbsp; natural disasters in Texas, and showed that in&nbsp;&nbsp; the early 2000s, the state had about 2.5 weather&nbsp; disasters per year with damage in the billions.&nbsp; By the mid-2020s, that number was up&nbsp; to 8.5 weather disasters per year.&nbsp; That’s had a serious impact not just on longtime&nbsp; residents in hurricane-prone areas, but also on&nbsp;&nbsp; the many newcomers who moved to Texas for the&nbsp; relatively affordable real estate. Now, many are&nbsp;&nbsp; facing extortionate insurance premiums, or worse&nbsp; - the prospect of insurance companies refusing to&nbsp;&nbsp; cover their homes at all in the coming year. Other areas of the state face high winds -&nbsp;&nbsp; Amarillo, Texas actually ranked as the windiest&nbsp; city in the United States. Due to the relative&nbsp;&nbsp; lack of vegetation and humidity in those regions,&nbsp; dust storms can be fierce - and that’s before we&nbsp;&nbsp; mention the air, but we’ll get to that soon. This is what Denton, Texas looks like&nbsp;&nbsp; in the middle of a dust storm. Strangely enough, the same state&nbsp;&nbsp; that is subject to droughts is also subject&nbsp; to floods. But these extremes also mean that&nbsp;&nbsp; water usage is sometimes restricted to&nbsp; make sure there’s enough for everyone.&nbsp; But at least during those blistering, often humid&nbsp; Texas summers - depending on where you are - you&nbsp;&nbsp; can count on the AC to keep you cool, right? Well… maybe.&nbsp; Texas has a unique electrical grid - and not in a&nbsp; good way. It’s mostly disconnected from the rest&nbsp;&nbsp; of the U.S., a decision rooted in the state’s&nbsp; fierce sense of independence… which apparently&nbsp;&nbsp; extends even to power lines. For those unfamiliar&nbsp; with why Texas is so obsessed with independence,&nbsp;&nbsp; here’s a bit of history: Texas was once part of&nbsp; Mexico, until it revolted and declared itself&nbsp;&nbsp; an independent republic. It remained a&nbsp; sovereign nation for nearly a decade,&nbsp;&nbsp; from March 2nd, 1836, to February 19th, 1846. Secession is still an issue often raised by some&nbsp;&nbsp; Texas politicians and citizens when federal&nbsp; politics don’t appear to be going their way.&nbsp;&nbsp; One 2024 poll by Redfield & Wilton Strategies&nbsp; found that 33% of Texans supported secession;&nbsp;&nbsp; while that’s not a majority, it’s&nbsp; certainly a significant amount.&nbsp; This history of independence has affected state&nbsp; politics up to this day, as Texans often feel&nbsp;&nbsp; they want to operate without much interference&nbsp; from the federal government. The phrase “don’t&nbsp;&nbsp; tread on me” is often used in political protests&nbsp; to signify Texas’ feelings of independence.&nbsp;&nbsp; However, it has also led to Texas wanting to&nbsp; maintain a lot of control of its own affairs,&nbsp;&nbsp; up to but not limited to, its power generation. Unfortunately, the Texas power grid has a habit of&nbsp;&nbsp; failing when it’s needed most - thanks in part to&nbsp; the ironically named Electric Reliability Council&nbsp;&nbsp; of Texas, or ERCOT. The grid especially tends to&nbsp; fail in extreme weather, which as we’ve already&nbsp;&nbsp; covered, Texas has plenty of. You may remember&nbsp; when the state made the news in 2021 for its&nbsp;&nbsp; entire grid failing for several days during&nbsp; Winter Storm Uri. A snowstorm hit the state,&nbsp;&nbsp; dumping 3-6 inches (7.6 - 15 cm) of snow on cities&nbsp; like Dallas and Austin. Maybe that doesn’t sound&nbsp;&nbsp; like a huge amount, but in a state that isn’t used&nbsp; to that kind of weather, peoples’ electricity,&nbsp;&nbsp; heating, and water shut down. Residents were left lighting fires&nbsp;&nbsp; and huddling under blankets for warmth.&nbsp; In the end, over 200 people died from a&nbsp;&nbsp; lack of utilities and freezing temperatures. From that moment, many Texans have been anxious&nbsp;&nbsp; about impending snow storms or cold fronts, urging&nbsp; each other to stock up on bottled water, food, and&nbsp;&nbsp; toilet paper, even in advance of below-freezing&nbsp; temperatures with no storm in sight. It’s not a&nbsp;&nbsp; great sign for your state when the thermometer&nbsp; hitting 25 degrees Fahrenheit (-4 degrees&nbsp;&nbsp; Celsius) turns citizens into survivalists. But surely the state government learned from&nbsp;&nbsp; their mistakes and have now done something after&nbsp; such a public failure of their power grid? Some&nbsp;&nbsp; people did try to by taking the electric company&nbsp; to court. Unfortunately, the court ruled that&nbsp;&nbsp; the privately-owned power grid in Texas has no&nbsp; obligation to provide power in times of crisis.&nbsp; And while Texas decided providing an unreliable&nbsp; grid wasn’t enough; they also deregulated their&nbsp;&nbsp; utility prices. While this isn’t bad for&nbsp; those with fixed rate energy contracts,&nbsp;&nbsp; some Texans still have variable rate contracts;&nbsp; meaning when it’s hard to provide power, utility&nbsp;&nbsp; companies will spike their prices up by a lot. For example, during Winter Storm Uri,&nbsp;&nbsp; the Public Utility Commission in Texas let&nbsp; the price of electricity rise to $9 per&nbsp;&nbsp; kilo-watt hour as opposed to the usual price of&nbsp; 12 cents per kilo-watt hour - a 7,400% increase.&nbsp; This means that the relatively cheap cost of&nbsp; housing in Texas – valued at a median price of&nbsp;&nbsp; $339,500 as of 2025 – may not be all that&nbsp; worth it. But let’s pivot away from the big&nbsp;&nbsp; picture issues with Texas for a moment to&nbsp; focus on something smaller… much smaller.&nbsp;&nbsp; And that would be the “Texas State Bird”, as some&nbsp; locals nickname it, also known as the cockroach.&nbsp; Did you know that Houston was ranked&nbsp; the dirtiest city in the U.S.? It earned&nbsp;&nbsp; the title for several reasons - high&nbsp; pollution levels, lack of cleanliness,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the cockroaches. The city ranks #1 nationwide&nbsp; for household cockroach infestations. And the&nbsp;&nbsp; infestations aren’t just limited to Houston…or&nbsp; even just cockroaches for that matter.&nbsp; There’s also massive beetles, mosquitoes the&nbsp; size of dragonflies, scorpions, fire ants,&nbsp;&nbsp; and tarantulas. As one Texan-born-and-raised&nbsp; commenter said online, “most of our fauna&nbsp;&nbsp; will bite, sting, or chase you up a tree”. But there must be another reason many people are&nbsp;&nbsp; moving here – including Elon Musk, who famously&nbsp; moved his headquarters to the state partly to&nbsp;&nbsp; avoid high California taxes. Maybe it’s the taxes? Because Texas has no state income tax!&nbsp;&nbsp; This may seem like everyone's hitting&nbsp; the free money jackpot. Except taxes&nbsp;&nbsp; still need to come from somewhere because a&nbsp; state, especially one as populous as Texas,&nbsp;&nbsp; still needs to function. So where does the&nbsp; money come from? The answer is, property taxes.&nbsp; Texas has some of the highest property taxes&nbsp; in the country, with a median of 1.67% and&nbsp;&nbsp; an average of 1.90% - compared to the U.S. median&nbsp; rate of 1.02%. That means the median annual Texas&nbsp;&nbsp; property tax bill comes out to just under $4,000,&nbsp; as opposed to the nationwide cost of $2,400.&nbsp; But that’s not the only high tax the state has -&nbsp; it also has one of the highest sales tax rates,&nbsp;&nbsp; amounting to 6.25%. Some local municipalities also&nbsp; add up to a 2% surcharge on that, meaning there&nbsp;&nbsp; are areas in Texas where you can end up paying&nbsp; 8.25% in sales tax on almost every purchase.&nbsp; That makes the statewide sales&nbsp; tax rank 13th across U.S. states,&nbsp;&nbsp; though that doesn’t account for the extra&nbsp; 2% many local areas and businesses impose.&nbsp; So even though there’s no state income&nbsp; tax, people may not end up saving much&nbsp;&nbsp; at all by moving to Texas, thanks to all&nbsp; the other hidden costs. Not to mention,&nbsp;&nbsp; the high property taxes being allocated to almost&nbsp; everything a state tax would generally be used&nbsp;&nbsp; for creating other problems. Mostly with schools. In most of the U.S., property taxes fund the local&nbsp;&nbsp; public school system. But in Texas, that money&nbsp; goes to a whole lot of things not covered by the&nbsp;&nbsp; non-existent income tax, including infrastructure,&nbsp; the public service sector, and healthcare.&nbsp; As a result, Texas ranks relatively low in public&nbsp; education, with persistent concerns about both&nbsp;&nbsp; quality and content. Over the past 15 years,&nbsp; the state has passed a series of controversial&nbsp;&nbsp; laws that strictly define what students can and&nbsp; cannot be taught - especially on topics that may&nbsp;&nbsp; conflict with strict Christian religious beliefs.&nbsp; In some cases, schools have even been instructed&nbsp;&nbsp; to present false or misleading information. One recent example was in early 2025,&nbsp;&nbsp; when the Texas Senate introduced two bills - one&nbsp; requiring the Ten Commandments to be displayed&nbsp;&nbsp; in every classroom, and another mandating&nbsp; dedicated time for prayer during the school day.&nbsp; When lawmakers opposed to the bill argued that&nbsp; this violates principles separating church and&nbsp;&nbsp; state, the Texas legislature responded by&nbsp; ignoring them and approving the bills. In&nbsp;&nbsp; defense of the bill, Senator Mayes Middleton&nbsp; said, “our schools are not God-free zones”.&nbsp; 166 religious leaders and representatives&nbsp; from Jewish, Muslim, Christian, Sikh,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Buddhist communities signed a letter&nbsp; protesting the Ten Commandments bill, but&nbsp;&nbsp; it did little to change the legislature’s mind. Texas also passed laws giving parents and school&nbsp;&nbsp; boards more power to ban books from school&nbsp; libraries. They emphasized that this was simply&nbsp;&nbsp; to prevent books that include profane content.&nbsp; But many educators fear that it will actually&nbsp;&nbsp; ban books that include any content relating to&nbsp; gender and sexuality that might be interpreted&nbsp;&nbsp; as going against Christian principles. And they have good reason to fear that,&nbsp;&nbsp; considering that Texas has also banned DEI&nbsp; initiatives across the state education system.&nbsp;&nbsp; This includes outlawing any training or programs&nbsp; that reference things like race, ethnicity,&nbsp;&nbsp; gender identity or sexual orientation - which&nbsp; many are wary will make life more difficult for&nbsp;&nbsp; students and educators from minority populations. One truth often echoed by Texas residents is that&nbsp;&nbsp; while the state can be a welcoming place for&nbsp; Christian believers, it can be much harder to&nbsp;&nbsp; navigate for those who aren’t religious&nbsp; - or aren’t Christian at all. This is&nbsp;&nbsp; especially true outside the major metro areas. PEN America found that Texas ranked third in&nbsp;&nbsp; the country when it comes to banned books, with&nbsp; twelve districts having banned a total of 538&nbsp;&nbsp; books. However, that’s not for a lack of trying.&nbsp; Parents in Texas challenge the availability of&nbsp;&nbsp; certain library books all the time, as Texas&nbsp; ranks first in the country for attempts at&nbsp;&nbsp; book banning. There were 93 separate attempts&nbsp; to ban over 2,300 book titles in 2022 alone.&nbsp; With all the book bans and state laws interfering&nbsp; with education, perhaps it’s no surprise that&nbsp;&nbsp; a study by WalletHub found that Texas is one&nbsp; of the least educated states in the country,&nbsp;&nbsp; ranking 41st. The state also ranks 49th when it&nbsp; comes to percentage of diploma holders in the U.S.&nbsp; While parents do have the option to send&nbsp; their children to charter or private schools,&nbsp;&nbsp; the latter are expensive, with an average private&nbsp; school costing $11,000 in tuition per year - with&nbsp;&nbsp; much higher fees in major cities like Dallas and&nbsp; Houston. So they are only really accessible to&nbsp;&nbsp; upper middle class and wealthy Texans, creating&nbsp; a big divide between rich and poor in who can and&nbsp;&nbsp; cannot afford decent schooling. Charter schools are free,&nbsp;&nbsp; but have problems of their own. Namely,&nbsp; they don’t seem to stay open for long.&nbsp; Between 2020 and 2022, 55 Texas charter schools&nbsp; closed. While the pandemic was ongoing then,&nbsp;&nbsp; students were still engaging in&nbsp; e-learning, and public schools didn’t&nbsp;&nbsp; experience anywhere near this closure rate. And the problem wasn’t just relegated to the&nbsp;&nbsp; pandemic years. San Antonio’s Jubilee Academies&nbsp; Highland Park closed just two weeks after the&nbsp;&nbsp; 2023 school year started. 210 students&nbsp; were left with nowhere to go, and parents&nbsp;&nbsp; scrambling last-minute for education solutions. Data shows that one in four charter schools&nbsp;&nbsp; fails within five years of opening, and within&nbsp; ten years, 40% of them fail overall. Reasons&nbsp;&nbsp; include low enrollment in almost half of cases,&nbsp; and fraud or mismanagement in 21.6% of cases.&nbsp; In other words, the average Texan parent is often&nbsp; stuck choosing between a struggling public school&nbsp;&nbsp; that mandates prayer, an expensive private&nbsp; school, or a charter school with a coin-flip&nbsp;&nbsp; chance of shutting down halfway through the&nbsp; year. None of the options are ideal - and for&nbsp;&nbsp; many families, it’s a constant gamble. At least they’re paying less in taxes?&nbsp; Well, whatever Texans do end up saving in taxes…&nbsp; they may end up paying in healthcare. Texas has&nbsp;&nbsp; an absurdly high number of uninsured residents,&nbsp; with 21.7% of adults lacking health insurance&nbsp;&nbsp; in 2023. That’s compared to the U.S. average&nbsp; of 11%. This makes Texas the state with the&nbsp;&nbsp; highest percentage of uninsured residents in&nbsp; the country. So logically, the state chose not&nbsp;&nbsp; to expand Medicare to cover them - something&nbsp; that happens when a state doesn’t like taxes.&nbsp; If you’re planning on moving, you better be&nbsp; prepared to pay for a great private plan, or&nbsp;&nbsp; have one provided to you through your job. You can&nbsp; go to one of the state’s public hospitals instead,&nbsp;&nbsp; but Texas public healthcare ranked 42nd out&nbsp; of all 50 states in 2020, so they may not be&nbsp;&nbsp; the best bet either. Unsurprising, since&nbsp; they don’t get much in the way of taxes.&nbsp; So a lot of people in Texas clearly&nbsp; don’t have decent healthcare access,&nbsp;&nbsp; and that’s not the best way to be because… Health in Texas isn’t great. The first problem&nbsp;&nbsp; is - shockingly enough, for a state that has a lot&nbsp; of wide-open expanses - the air quality. Texas is&nbsp;&nbsp; the heart of the U.S. oil and gas industry, and&nbsp; a lot of places that rely on those industries,&nbsp;&nbsp; particularly in West Texas, rank as some&nbsp; of the most polluted cities in the country.&nbsp; For people with asthma and other&nbsp; breathing issues, living in&nbsp;&nbsp; Texas, and particularly West Texas, is difficult. 2.3 million people in Texas live within half a&nbsp;&nbsp; mile (0.8 km) of oil and gas operations, spewing&nbsp; more than 8,500 tons (7.7 million kg) of pollution&nbsp;&nbsp; in the air, including substances like benzene,&nbsp; formaldehyde, methane, and plenty of others. A&nbsp;&nbsp; huge segment of West Texas, including most of the&nbsp; counties around Odessa and Midland, has a cancer&nbsp;&nbsp; risk that “exceeds the EPA’s level of concern”. And if that weren’t enough, Dallas ranked as&nbsp;&nbsp; the second worst city in the U.S. for allergy&nbsp; sufferers in 2023, thanks to sky-high pollen&nbsp;&nbsp; counts and the state’s generally dusty conditions.&nbsp; In fact, many people who move to Texas develop&nbsp;&nbsp; allergies for the first time in their lives. And there are far more deadly things to worry&nbsp;&nbsp; about than sneezing - like cancer. In Odessa,&nbsp; for example, which was mostly built up for&nbsp;&nbsp; oil industry workers, the amount of pollution&nbsp; in the air is staggering. Air analysts found&nbsp;&nbsp; that hydrogen sulfide levels were 82 times their&nbsp; acceptable levels for residential areas, making&nbsp;&nbsp; everything smell like rotten eggs in addition&nbsp; to slowly killing you from the inside out.&nbsp; In fact, residents are strongly&nbsp; advised to not even drink the water.&nbsp; But that’s far from Texas’ only problem. It has,&nbsp; perhaps unsurprisingly, some of the unhealthiest&nbsp;&nbsp; cities in the country. In 2023, the state had an&nbsp; obesity rate of 35.5%, which isn’t the worst in&nbsp;&nbsp; the country, but is also far from the best,&nbsp; putting it at number 18 nationwide. In 2021,&nbsp;&nbsp; the state was also ranked 18th nationwide for&nbsp; age-adjusted death rates. Local residents also&nbsp;&nbsp; had an almost 4% higher incidence of heart&nbsp; disease than the general U.S. population.&nbsp; Lack of healthcare access, combined with&nbsp; food deserts, a heavy meat-centric cuisine,&nbsp;&nbsp; environmental pollutants, and an intense&nbsp; car culture that leads to people living&nbsp;&nbsp; pretty sedentary lives, have caused&nbsp; relatively shorter lifespans in Texas.&nbsp;&nbsp; Residents live an average of 76.5 years, as&nbsp; opposed to the U.S. average of 77.4 years.&nbsp; Speaking of car culture, there’s one expense&nbsp; most Texans can’t avoid - owning a car and paying&nbsp;&nbsp; for gas. Even in massive cities like Houston and&nbsp; Dallas, public transportation options are limited.&nbsp;&nbsp; As a result, 93% of households in Harris County&nbsp; - where Houston is located - and 95.4% in Dallas&nbsp;&nbsp; own a car - placing them among the highest&nbsp; car ownership rates in any U.S. metro area.&nbsp; In contrast, major metropolitan areas like Chicago&nbsp; and Washington D.C. only had 72.5% and 62.7% of&nbsp;&nbsp; vehicles with car ownership. That’s not reflective&nbsp; of an income discrepancy between the various&nbsp;&nbsp; cities. Instead it highlights the inability to&nbsp; get around Dallas and Houston without a vehicle.&nbsp; Since Texas was mostly developed after&nbsp; the car came into common use, even the&nbsp;&nbsp; big cities are pretty car-centric.&nbsp; While you can get around by bus,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s not the easiest thing to do in the state’s&nbsp; sprawling metro areas - subways are non-existent,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the light rail systems serving areas like&nbsp; Houston and Austin don’t entice too many locals.&nbsp; The good news is, gas in Texas is usually&nbsp; pretty cheap thanks to the state producing&nbsp;&nbsp; a lot of it locally. The average price&nbsp; at the pump is $2.76 per gallon, vs. a&nbsp;&nbsp; nationwide average of $3.18 as of late May 2025. The bad news is that Texans still end up burning&nbsp;&nbsp; through a lot of gas - so much so that any savings&nbsp; at the pump can quickly be wiped out by how much&nbsp;&nbsp; time is spent idling in traffic or driving&nbsp; long distances. And forget about walking as an&nbsp;&nbsp; alternative. In many big city neighborhoods, it’s&nbsp; almost impossible to get around on foot. Urban and&nbsp;&nbsp; suburban areas are so car-centric that crossing&nbsp; a main street might require walking hundreds of&nbsp;&nbsp; feet just to find a safe intersection. In fact,&nbsp; some main streets don’t even have sidewalks.&nbsp; Not to mention, since Texas is the&nbsp; second biggest state in the country,&nbsp;&nbsp; the distances from one place to another are often&nbsp; enormous. So, Texans absolutely need a car - and&nbsp;&nbsp; preferably one that can handle the horrible&nbsp; weather conditions and less than great roads&nbsp;&nbsp; in the deep country - to get around the state. As you might imagine in big cities where getting&nbsp;&nbsp; around by car is the only option, traffic in&nbsp; places like Houston, Austin, and Dallas can be&nbsp;&nbsp; intense. Gridlock has become a severe problem,&nbsp; with Houston now ranking in the top ten cities&nbsp;&nbsp; nationwide for traffic during rush hour commutes.&nbsp; The average commute in Houston was 29.8 minutes,&nbsp;&nbsp; just under Los Angeles at 30.1 minutes -&nbsp; notoriously one of the largest and most&nbsp;&nbsp; congested cities in the country. The&nbsp; average commute in Dallas was slightly&nbsp;&nbsp; better - but not by much - at 27.8 minutes. Though the state – and city governments – assure&nbsp;&nbsp; Texas that they are working on great&nbsp; public transportation initiatives,&nbsp;&nbsp; most Texans would like to see it to believe&nbsp; it. And it does nothing to change the fact&nbsp;&nbsp; that traffic right now is a nightmare. This all sounds like a bad state of affairs&nbsp;&nbsp; so far – why aren’t residents doing anything&nbsp; about it? Surely they can vote for the change&nbsp;&nbsp; they believe in? Many Texans are perfectly fine&nbsp; with how things are going. And those who do want&nbsp;&nbsp; to change state regulations and laws with their&nbsp; vote may have a tough time, as Texas has been&nbsp;&nbsp; leading the charge in voter suppression laws. Texan Senate Bill 1 was introduced to make life&nbsp;&nbsp; more difficult for many Texans who wanted to vote.&nbsp; A series of hoops were put in place for those who&nbsp;&nbsp; want to vote by mail, and help for people with&nbsp; disabilities or language barriers was severely&nbsp;&nbsp; restricted - which some people argued targeted&nbsp; specific political demographics. Unfortunately,&nbsp;&nbsp; the bill became a law in 2021. However, a&nbsp; federal judge ruled parts of it illegal,&nbsp;&nbsp; since they violate the Voting Rights Act. Though there are certainly a lot of advantages&nbsp;&nbsp; to living in Texas, many people are unaware of the&nbsp; hidden costs and realities of life in the state. Thanks for joining me today! I hope you&nbsp; enjoyed the video, and if you want to see&nbsp;&nbsp; more videos like this, then please consider&nbsp; subscribing and turn on notifications,&nbsp;&nbsp; really does make a difference. Thanks again,&nbsp; and I'll see you next time on Map Pack!

[Peter] You said your son and his boyfriend? -Uh-huh. -And I think most people from the outside would say, "That would be super frowned upon down here." What are your thoughts on that? -No, I'm happy you asked that because I'd like the opportunity to address that. I think when my son was younger he didn't come out as gay until he was older but I mean I always sort of suspected. But I've always wanted him to be true and authentic to himself and so many times people that are homosexual, they live in a community like this, they wait until their mother's dead. Especially gay guys tell me this all the time. That they couldn't come out because they didn't want to disappoint their mother. And my son and I are really close. He was born on my birthday. So we're of the same zodiac sign and everything. So I think we're very similar personality wise So I would I always say, "You know I love you no matter what." I'd tell the kids and they'd say, "If we did this would you love us?". -Right, right. -And I was doing that for his benefit. He laughs now and tells me, "I know what you were doing." but I think it was harder when he was younger here but I'm very proud of the area that they have a much larger support group for homosexuals now, and the gay community, and that's great. Much more accepting of that lifestyle and people being authentic to who they are. Daniel felt… I think he felt like he had to move to New York to be accepted. And now when he comes home… And he couldn't wait to leave here. But now when he comes home he loves it and realizes what he had here. And I think he's much more comfortable here but I think the whole community in general has changed a lot over the last ten years. Certainly over the last 20 years. I think that the South in general, not just Alabama but the South in general is portrayed a certain way in movies and whatnot of a close-minded communities, and very judgmental, and racist, and you know… In a negative connotation and I don't believe that to be true. I think that it works for Hollywood. Doesn't matter who you are or what you believe and you know, I prefer not to talk about politics at family functions ever because there are both sides at the dinner table and usually causes a problem when we bring it up. -So I just-- -Well everything's divided these days. -I think that the media has created a further divide between people because I don't think that really exists. I mean I think maybe small pockets of it but in general… But I think the media feeds that. -Oh, 100% because the reality on the ground, I'm all over the country, all different cultures, urban, rural, I'm not seeing the friction at all, okay? But then if you get online or watch TV, "Wow, we're all at each other." It's not like that and it's unfortunate when people don't travel they can't see it for themselves. So they're believing that's the reality. -Well I believe that to be true. I think that traveling is the best medicine to create more togetherness. -You know, embracing each other as human beings and none of the other stuff really matters but when you travel and realise that people live differently than you were brought up in the area you live. Certainly the area that I grew up in. I mean travel opens your eyes to everything and it makes you more tolerant to all different kinds of life and all different kinds of people. And Doug, did you plan the canoe to come in at this exact time? -I was just thanking for Bruce to not only pay for the ducks but to pay for these folks. -There we go. So this is Bayside Academy. It's a private school here in our area and it's right here on the bay. Not many people can say they go to school on Mobile Bay. Some children arrive to school in their boats. Their parent's boats, they drop them off at the pier and they have class out on the pier and then they'll come out with a seine net and dredge, and then take it to the science department and do science experiments on the things they get out of the bay. -Wow, what an education. -I don't know if Doug told you about this but we have jubilees. Which is when there's a lack of oxygen in the water, and the shrimp, and crab, and everything come to the surface. That happens when the moon is just right and the tides are just right. We're only one of two places in the world that that happens. -It's a natural phenomenon. -And there's a bell in their courtyard where they would ring the bell years ago to alert all the neighbors that there was a jubilee happening, and everyone would come out with their ice chests and just gather it up because you can stand at the shore and just take nets, and fill your cooler with shrimp and crab. -Oh, that's great. Guys, thank you, locals, for bringing us in. -You're welcome, thanks for coming. -Thanks for dropping in. You've got a good reason to come back, we've got a beach that's a whole different area. -Yeah, we didn't go to that, 30 miles away. -April, mullet toss, Floribama. -It's Mardi Gras on the beach. And if someone's coming here they need to speak with you, correct? -Or me. -Oh, we have two realtors in the house. -Yeah, Doug Kilborn and Michelle Bigler. We're both with the same company, Wise Living in Fairhope. Those links are down below. -Thank you so much. -Thank you, brother, it was a pleasure. -My pleasure. -Thanks for bringing us in, it brings understanding and gives us a visual of what things are like 'cause it's hard to imagine without this. -One more thing… Roll Tide. -Huh? -Roll Tide. -What's Roll Tide? -University of Alabama Crimson Tide, best football team in the country. [Michelle laughs] -College football, that's big here? -It's a lifestyle. -It is. -I think Mississippi's gonna get you this year. - Texas’s RICHEST Neighborhoods Are NEXT LEVEL 🤯: Kind: captions Language: en Have you ever stopped to think about where the wealthiest people in Texas actually live? You are about to enter places where luxury feels normal and success shows up in everyday life with huge homes, spotless streets, and neighborhoods built for people who have reached the top and stayed there. In these communities, comfort, privacy, and status quietly stand out without having to stress yourself. Sit back and let us explore Texas neighborhoods where success feels built into the ground beneath your feet. One, River Oaks, Houston. River Oaks is not just another wealthy neighborhood. It represents the essence of old money in Texas. From the moment you enter River Oaks, one can easily sense a change in atmosphere. The roads are wide and meticulously maintained, lined with grand oak trees that provide natural shade. It feels distinctly separate from the busy life of Houston, even though downtown is very close by. There are no flashy signs, no commercial noise, and definitely no hurry. Everything in River Oaks is designed to promote a more relaxed lifestyle, where privacy and control are prioritized over being seen. The residences in River Oaks are set back from the street, often hidden behind decorative iron gates, strong stone walls, or thick hedges. Many of these homes look more like impressive European estates than typical American houses. You'll find houses like Italian villas, Georgian style homes, French chateaus, and custom-built properties that can take years to complete. These are not just houses meant for quick sales. They are homes meant to last, often passed down through families or owned by those who have no intention of selling. This is part of the reason prices range from $1 million to over $20 million with a median value around $3 million. The high cost per square foot isn't about being flashy, it's about the land, the location, and the exclusivity. River Oaks has strong ties to Houston's oil and energy heritage. Many residents built their fortunes during the oil booms of the 20th century and reinvested that wealth into energy infrastructure, pipelines, private equity, and industrial projects. Billionaires like Richard Kinder exemplify this legacy today. What truly sets River Oaks apart is that the wealth here does not seek attention. Residents value their privacy. It's common to live next to someone worth hundreds of millions and never Also, social life in River Oaks revolves around private institutions rather than public spaces. The River Oaks Country Club is a central pillar of the community, offering golf, tennis, fine dining, and networking that quietly influences Houston's business and political landscape. Membership is selective and often inherited, reinforcing the neighborhood's closed nature. Schools in and around River Oaks are among the best funded in Texas, supported by high property taxes and private donations, giving residents elite education options without needing private boarding schools. Security and governance are another defining feature. River Oaks benefits from dedicated patrols, strong neighborhood associations, and zoning laws that strictly limit development. This prevents high-density housing, commercial intrusion, or architectural inconsistency. The result is stability. While other luxury neighborhoods rise and fall with market trends, River Oaks remains insulated from volatility. Just west of River Oaks is a different kind of wealthy neighborhood. Here, people don't rely on old family mansions. Instead, their status comes from being independent, owning lots of land, and keeping their suburban area peaceful and well-kept. Two, Memorial Villages, Houston. The Memorial Villages represent a unique form of wealth, one centered on control, independence, and a peaceful suburban lifestyle. Made of six small, self-governing districts, these villages function almost like private cities within Houston. Each village has its own local government, zoning regulations, police department, and distinct character. This independence is a major reason wealthy families choose to live here. It allows residents to protect property values, regulate development, and maintain an environment that prioritizes peace and space. Driving through the Memorial Villages offers a stark contrast to River Oaks. Instead of tightly packed estates and manicured gardens, you find larger plots of land, meandering roads, and abundant natural greenery. Homes are typically situated on acre-sized lots, encircled by tall trees and expansive lawns. The vibe is more laid-back, yet still affluent. Many residences are modern custom designs, tailored for families seeking privacy without the rigidity of traditional estates. While median home prices surpass $2.4 million, this figure alone doesn't capture the complete picture. What truly characterizes the Memorial Villages is the concentration of income. Neighborhoods like Hunters Creek Village boast per capita incomes that compete with the wealthiest towns in the United States. The residents include energy executives, medical professionals, corporate attorneys, and entrepreneurs who prefer discretion over ostentation. Unlike luxury areas driven by trends, the Memorial Villages draw individuals looking for long-term stability, often raising several generations within the same community. Nature plays a huge role in the appeal of this neighborhood. Memorial Park, one of the largest urban parks in the US, is adjacent to several villages and features extensive trails, golf courses, and green areas. Bayous and protected land corridors help reduce traffic noise and visual distractions, creating a calm that feels rare in a major city. This closeness to nature, combined with proximity to Houston's business districts, creates a balance that appeals to high-income professionals. Education is another vital aspect. The Memorial Villages are served by some of the top public schools in Texas. These schools enjoy excellent funding, strong parental engagement, and academic results that compete with private schools. This enables families to obtain high-quality education while maintaining community stability. Zoning regulations in the Memorial Villages are strict. Commercial developments are limited and have strict restrictions on apartment buildings and enforced architectural standards. These regulations are intentional. They aim to maintain the area's character and safeguard long-term wealth. Residents are actively involved in local governance, ensuring that decisions reflect their priorities. While Houston's wealth thrives behind trees and district borders, a new kind of fortune has been reshaping the hills just outside Austin. Three, Westlake Hills, Austin. Westlake Hills represents the modern evolution of Texas wealth, where technology, innovation, and rapid financial growth have reshaped an already desirable landscape. Located just west of downtown Austin, this area sits high above the city, offering panoramic views of Lake Austin, rolling hills, and expansive skies. The setting alone signals exclusivity. Roads curve with the terrain, homes are elevated for privacy, and the entire area feels intentionally removed from the city below, even though it is only minutes away. Unlike older wealth centers built on oil or land ownership, Westlake Hills is fueled by tech money. Executives, startup founders, venture capitalists, and C-suite leaders dominate the population. Many residents relocated from California, attracted by Texas's tax advantages and Austin's growing tech ecosystem. This migration pushed home prices rapidly upward, with estates now commonly ranging from $2 million to $15 million. The surge in millionaire households has been dramatic, transforming Westlake Hills into one of the most income-dense areas in the state. The architecture here reflects modern priorities. Homes are often sleek, minimalist, and custom-built, emphasizing glass walls, open layouts, and seamless indoor-outdoor living. Infinity pools, rooftop decks, and private docks are common features. Unlike River Oaks or Memorial Villages, where tradition dominates, Westlake Hills embraces innovation and reinvention. Many homes are demolished and rebuilt, reflecting the fast-paced mindset of their owners. Education plays a central role in the area's appeal. Westlake Hills is zoned to the highly regarded Eanes Independent School District, consistently ranked among the best in Texas. For tech executives raising families, this offers elite public education without the need for private schools. This combination of top-tier schools and extreme wealth creates intense competition for housing, further driving prices upward. Despite its modern feel, privacy remains a top priority. Streets are quiet, security is discreet, and zoning laws limit density. There are no large commercial centers, no nightlife districts, and no public attractions that draw crowds. Residents can live highly public professional lives while maintaining personal anonymity at home. Westlake Hills carries wealth in motion. It is less about inheritance and more about timing, risk, and growth. Fortunes here are newer, but no less powerful. Just like north of the wealthy tech hill country, there's a large, carefully planned community that combines high-powered business life with luxurious, resort-like living. Four, The Woodlands, Houston area. The Woodlands is a different vision of wealth in Texas, one built around scale, planning, and lifestyle engineering, rather than historic prestige or urban proximity. Located north of Houston, this massive master-planned community spans roughly 28,000 acres, making it feel less like a neighborhood and more like a privately curated city for the affluent. From the moment you enter The Woodlands, the intention is clear. This is a place designed to make everyday life feel effortless for people with money. One of the defining features of The Woodlands is space. Unlike older luxury neighborhoods where land is limited, abundance is part of the appeal. Wide roads, deep setbacks, lakes, forests, and miles of trails give residents room to breathe. Nature is not decorative, it is foundational. Trees were preserved during development, not cleared, giving the area a calm, resort-like feel. This natural environment attracts high-earning professionals who want luxury without density. Wealth in The Woodlands is closely tied to corporate power, especially energy. Major companies like ExxonMobil established large campuses nearby, anchoring a population of executives, engineers, and senior managers. This has created a stable, high-income base that supports luxury housing across many price levels. While some homes start around $400,000, elite gated sections like Carlton Woods push prices into the $5 to $10 million range, especially for properties overlooking golf courses or private lakes. Carlton Woods is the crown jewel of The Woodlands. This gated enclave is home to CEOs, private equity partners, and global executives who value security and discretion. Golf courses designed by Jack Nicklaus and Tom Fazio serve as both recreational spaces and informal networking grounds. Memberships are expensive, selective, and socially influential. Life here revolves around private clubs, not public venues. Another key factor is infrastructure. The Woodlands was designed with self-sufficiency in mind. Residents have access to high-end shopping, fine dining, medical centers, performance venues, and business parks without needing to drive into Houston. This convenience is a major draw for wealthy families who value time as much as money. It allows residents to live, work, and socialize within a controlled environment. What sets The Woodlands apart is its balance. It offers luxury without intensity, wealth without noise, and status without spectacle. It is a place where corporate success translates into comfort, routine, and long-term stability, rather than display. While The Woodlands shows how wealth can be planned and managed at scale, North Texas offers an older, more traditional expression of elite living, one built on independence, exclusivity, and quiet authority. Five, Highland Park, Dallas. Highland Park is one of the most powerful symbols of wealth in Texas, not because of size or flash, but because of control. This small, independent town is completely surrounded by Dallas, yet operates under its own government, school system, police force, and services. That independence is the foundation of its prestige. For the wealthy, Highland Park offers something priceless, the ability to live inside a major city while remaining separate from it. From a physical standpoint, Highland Park is polished and intentional. Streets are clean, landscaping is manicured, and architecture follows a refined aesthetic. Homes range from Spanish Revival and Mediterranean estates to modern mansions, many priced between $2.4 million and $10 million. While prices are high, the real value lies in location, governance, and stability, rather than sheer square footage. Education is one of Highland Park's greatest advantages. Highland Park ISD is consistently ranked among the best public school systems in Texas, and even the country, supported by high property taxes and significant parental involvement, the schools rival elite private institutions. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle. Wealthy families move in for the schools, and the schools improve because of the wealth. Wealth in Highland Park is tied to corporate leadership, finance, and legacy business interests. Executives from companies like AT&T, investment firms, and family-owned enterprises dominate the population. Unlike tech hubs, where fortunes can be volatile, Highland Park's wealth is often steady and multi-generational. Many families have lived here for decades, reinforcing a strong internal culture. Retail and social life reflect this refinement. Highland Park Village, one of the first planned shopping centers in the US, serves as a luxury hub with brands like Hermès, Chanel, and Dior. Unlike flashy malls, it feels restrained and elegant, designed to serve residents rather than tourists. Shopping here is less about consumption and more about continuity. Security and governance are tightly managed. Highland Park has its own police and fire departments, ensuring rapid response and community-focused enforcement. Zoning laws are strict, preventing high-density housing or commercial sprawl. These controls protect property values and preserve the town's character, making Highland Park one of the most stable luxury markets in the state. Highland Park is not experimental. It does not chase trends or reinvent itself. Its power comes from consistency, tradition, and quiet confidence. This is wealth that knows exactly who it is and has no reason to change. Within Dallas lies an exclusive area that values size over tradition, where sprawling modern mansions rise, privacy is deep, and influence quietly thrives behind gates and long driveways. Six, Preston Hollow, Dallas. Preston Hollow is a more expensive and less formal version of Dallas wealth, where space, privacy, and modern influence matter more than tradition or town boundaries. Located north of downtown Dallas, this neighborhood is not an independent city like Highland Park, but in many ways, it operates with the same level of exclusivity. What separates Preston Hollow is scale. Lots are larger, streets are quieter, and homes are built to feel like private compounds rather than showpieces. Home prices typically range from $2 million to $8 million, with some estates exceeding that. Architecture varies widely, from Mediterranean-style mansions to sleek contemporary builds. Unlike Highland Park, Preston Hollow allows more architectural freedom, which attracts residents who want custom-designed homes rather than traditional aesthetics. Driving through Preston Hollow, the first thing that stands out is distance. Houses sit far apart, often hidden behind tall hedges, iron gates, and long driveways. Many properties include guest houses, home gyms, pools, private offices, and even small security stations. This layout appeals to executives and public figures who want to live close to the city's business core while remaining unseen. It is common for residents to go days without interacting with neighbors, not out of isolation, but by design. The wealth in Preston Hollow is strongly tied to modern corporate leadership and entrepreneurship. CEOs, private equity partners, hedge fund managers, and major real estate developers dominate the area. Former residents like George W. Bush and Mark Cuban highlight the neighborhood's appeal to powerful individuals who value both influence and privacy. This is not old inherited wealth in the classic sense, but wealth that has already matured and stabilized. Education plays a quieter, but still important role. While Preston Hollow does not have its own school district, the surrounding public schools benefit heavily from parental funding, political influence, and private support. In practice, this creates a public school experience that mirrors private education, especially when combined with elite private schools nearby. For wealthy families, this flexibility is a major advantage. Security in Preston Hollow is largely private. Many homes employ personal security teams, advanced surveillance systems, and controlled access points. The neighborhood itself is not heavily policed in a visible way, but crime rates remain low due to deterrents and awareness. This low-profile approach aligns with the neighborhood's philosophy, protection without attention. Preston Hollow is also strategically located. Residents can reach downtown Dallas, major business districts, and private airports quickly. This accessibility makes it ideal for people whose schedules involve frequent travel and high-level meetings. Life here is efficient, controlled, and intentionally separated from public visibility. Preston Hollow represents wealth that has moved beyond prestige and into autonomy. It is not about being seen in the right places, but about having the freedom to live exactly how one wants. And while Dallas wealth stretches outward into quiet compounds, San Antonio offers a very different kind of luxury, one shaped by history, restraint, and generational continuity. Seven, Alamo Heights, San Antonio. Alamo Heights is one of those understated wealthy neighborhoods in Texas, defined not by size or spectacle, but its rich history and a sense of discretion. Just a quick 5-mile jaunt from downtown San Antonio, this small, independent city has been a home for military leaders, business moguls, and families whose wealth has quietly grown over the years. Unlike the bustling tech or energy hotspots, Alamo Heights prioritizes stability over rapid expansion, and this ethic is reflected in its physical surroundings. The streets are cozy and shaded by mature trees, lined with older homes that have been lovingly preserved instead of torn down. Many of these houses have been around for decades, not fit the bill of modern luxury, but their true worth lies in their prime location, craftsmanship, and the land they occupy. While average home prices may be lower than those in Houston or Dallas, the estates in the most sought-after areas can easily climb into the millions. In Alamo Heights, wealth is often linked to sectors like defense, health care, finance, and local business ownership. The close proximity to military bases and major headquarters like USAA has significantly influenced the community's character. Many residents are retired officers, executives, or professionals who appreciate order, structure, and a bit of privacy. This creates a serene, disciplined vibe that sets it apart from the more ostentatious luxury neighborhoods. Education is another strong pillar of Alamo Heights. The local school district consistently ranks among the best in Texas. High academic standards, strong community engagement, and cherished traditions. Families are often drawn to these schools, with generations attending the same institutions, which helps to strengthen those deep-rooted community connections. Social life in Alamo Heights revolves around private clubs and long-established institutions. Places like the San Antonio Country Club and the Argyle Club serve as social anchors, where relationships are built quietly over decades. Membership is selective, and status is communicated through association rather than display. Governance and independence also matter greatly. As its own city, Alamo Heights controls zoning, development, and services. This autonomy protects the neighborhood from overdevelopment and preserves its character. Commercial intrusion is limited, and large apartment complexes are rare. Alamo Heights is not trying to compete with Houston or Austin. It does not chase billionaire status or rapid growth. Instead, it offers stability, respectability, and a deeply rooted sense of place. For families who value tradition, safety, and quiet success, this restraint is its greatest luxury. But for those who want complete isolation, tight security, and resort-level exclusivity, San Antonio hides one final enclave where wealth withdraws even further from public view. Eight, The Dominion, San Antonio. The Dominion is one of Texas's most exclusive luxury communities, built specifically for privacy. Situated north of San Antonio, this gated area covers over 1,400 acres and has fewer than 600 homes. Access is strictly regulated, and security is a key aspect. Once you enter, the outside world feels distant. Homes in The Dominion are priced from $1 million to more than $4 million, with many offering hilltop views, infinity pools, and unique designs. The streets are peaceful, traffic is minimal, and neighbors are thoroughly vetted. Residents include defense contractors, developers, medical executives, and business owners who value privacy over fame. The Dominion Country Club is the heart of community life, providing golf, dining, and private events. Social interactions here are deliberate, not casual. Residents decide when and how to connect. What makes The Dominion unique is its lack of desire for attention. There are no tourist spots, shopping areas, or public access. It serves solely as a secure haven for the affluent. The Dominion shows how luxury in Texas can be completely shielded behind gates. In contrast, Austin presents a different kind of wealth, one that harmonizes nature, creativity, and influence just outside the city center. Nine, Barton Creek. Barton Creek is one of the most luxurious neighborhoods in Austin. It is large, gated, and surrounded by rolling hills and plenty of green space, giving it a serene, resort-like atmosphere. Homes in this area typically range from $2 million to $8 million, and are often quite spacious, featuring modern designs, large yards, and luxurious amenities. Many residences are located near private golf courses and country clubs, allowing residents to enjoy sports, swimming, and other recreational activities without leaving the neighborhood. The community also offers beautiful walking paths, scenic overlooks, and quiet areas perfect for outdoor gatherings or evening strolls. Wildlife is often visible in the green spaces, adding a natural charm to daily life. The streets are tranquil and lined with trees, contributing to a peaceful and private environment. Barton Creek attracts tech executives, business owners, and investors seeking safety, comfort, and privacy while remaining close to downtown Austin. Residents enjoy access to private gyms, clubs, and social gatherings, fostering a lifestyle that blends relaxation with prestige. With its stunning homes, picturesque surroundings, and high-end facilities, Barton Creek stands out as one of Austin's most esteemed neighborhoods, ideal for those desiring luxury, privacy, and convenience all in one location. The area's strong sense of community and well-maintained amenities make it appealing for families as well as professionals looking for an elegant yet comfortable lifestyle. 10. Tarrytown. Tarrytown is a lovely, historic area located just a short distance from west of downtown Austin. Homes here usually cost between $2 million and $6 million, reflecting the neighborhood's popularity and high demand. What makes Tarrytown special is the mix of long-standing Austin families and new tech millionaires, creating a unique combination of history and modern wealth. Although the houses might be smaller than those in Barton Creek, they have a unique charm, often featuring classic designs along with modern updates and stylish interiors. Many homes also have beautiful gardens, outdoor seating areas, or small yards that make the neighborhood feel warm and inviting. Tarrytown also has parks, green areas, and boutique shops that add to its welcoming, vibrant feel. Even with increasing property taxes, people are still keen to find homes here, attracted by the ideal mix of prestige, history, and location. The streets are filled with mature trees, providing a peaceful, shaded atmosphere that makes walking or biking through the area enjoyable. The community has a friendly, close-knit feel, with neighbors often meeting at local events, parks, or community gatherings. Local cafes, small galleries, and weekend markets add charm and encourage social interaction. Additionally, being so near to downtown Austin allows residents easy access to a variety of restaurants, shops, cultural sites, offices, and entertainment options, making city living both convenient and enjoyable. For anyone wanting to experience a lively city lifestyle while living in a calm, beautiful, and historically rich neighborhood, Tarrytown is a fantastic option. Its combination of history, convenience, and community spirit makes it one of Austin's most desirable places to live. Texas's richest neighborhoods don't match most people's expectations. They are serene, quiet, and often go unnoticed. Whether it's the old oil money in Houston, corporate power in Dallas, tech wealth in Austin, or the structured lifestyle in San Antonio, one thing stands out. True wealth in Texas values privacy over performance. If you found this deep dive of Texas's top neighborhoods interesting, drop a comment below about which neighborhood surprised you the most and which one you'd pick to live in if money were no object. Also, remember to like the video, subscribe to the channel, and share it with anyone interested in understanding how wealth operates behind the scenes. Thank you for watching, and see you in the next video.

- The Strange New Status Symbol in Modern Luxury Homes: Kind: captions Language: en 10 years ago, if you walked through a luxury home, the status symbols were obvious. A wine cellar, a dramatic two-story foyer, a beautiful formal dining room with an elaborate covered ceiling. It was luxury design centered around adult prestige spaces that screamed, "We've made it." Now, some of the most expensive new builds are dedicating prime square footage to something much stranger. Bunk beds. Not in a cabin, not in a vacation rental. We're talking three, four, $5 million homes with custom mill work, integrated lighting, storage cubbies, and space for four or even six children. In today's video on homes explained, I want to unpack why that shift is happening and what it reveals about how modern luxury homes are being designed and the values they're starting to prioritize. Because this isn't really about sleeping space, it's about what modern luxury is trying to signal. Over the past couple of years, I've noticed very high-end builders intentionally building large bonus rooms with an entire wall dedicated to elaborate built-in bunks. Now, these are full scale, totally decked out, usually painted like a nice bold blue or green, integrated sconces, iPad holders, storage drawers, and individual niches. Symmetrical and carefully curated, this feels less like a spontaneous sleepover and more like a page from the Pottery Barn kids catalog. [snorts] But here's what fascinates me. Because children are not leading this design trend, most kids would happily throw a sleeping bag on the floor and call it a sleepover. They don't need custom mill work and integrated lighting to feel excited about having friends over. So, if it's not about practicality, what is it about? And this is where design starts to tell a bigger story. Many of the families building or buying these luxury homes with the built-in bunks are now generation X, which is a wonderful generation. But we grew up in a very different environment. Childhood was largely spent outdoors, very loosely supervised at best, and certainly uncurated compared to today. You rode bikes, you went home when the street lights came on. Social life happened organically. Today, that kind of spontaneous neighborhood culture is far less common. Schedules are tighter, socializing is structured, and screen time has taken over from physical proximity. And so, the home starts to compensate. And rather than seeing a bunch of bikes on the front lawn, we build the hangout space inside the house. Instead of unplanned sleepovers, we create a beautifully designed backdrop specifically for them. And that's where the bunk wall becomes interesting because the bonus room with a wall of built-in bunks isn't just a sleeping arrangement. It's a signal. It suggests that this home was designed for gathering, for cousins piling in, for friends staying over, for noise activity in shared space. It's connection by design. Because when togetherness is no longer as organic as it once was, we begin to build it into the floor plan. In essence, architecture is doing some of the social work. Luxury used to emphasize comfort and display, but now increasingly it emphasizes experience. And as we see here, not just adult experience, that sounds dodgy, but you know what I mean, but shared family experience. The floor plan itself is being shaped around the idea of togetherness. And then there's another layer to this. Take for a moment and consider a model home. One of the most powerful elements isn't the square footage. It's not the finishes. It's the promise. the suggestion and the appeal of this new perfect house is how we're going to live going forward. It offers a fresh start, a reset, and people see it as an upgraded version of their own lives. In the same way, an elaborate bunk wall doesn't just provide extra beds. It conveys connection. It suggests that this is a house where friends will gather and where cousins will stay over and where togetherness is expected rather than incidental. Whether or not it's used every weekend doesn't matter. It signals intention. It says we want this kind of life for our children. There's also the unspoken optimism built into these spaces. The idea that if we create the perfect setting, the perfect backdrop, then the social life will surely follow. that if we build it, they will come philosophy. Perhaps this reflects a belief that architecture can encourage the kind of friendships and memories we want our children to have. And for many of us, this may also be shaped by our own childhoods, what they look like, or probably more realistically, what they didn't look like. And that's what makes this trend so interesting from an architectural standpoint. Homes don't just reflect how we live, they reflect what we hope for. But every era leaves a design signature. And in the 1990s traditional home, it was the built-in desk, the so-called mother's desk. In the mid200s, it was the fully enclosed theater room with the tiered seating. And in the modern farmhouse, it was the shiplap and the barn door. These features aren't random. They capture a moment. And the very elaborate bunk wall in a 2023 to 26 luxury new build may be doing exactly the same thing. It's not just a sleeping arrangement. It could also be a time stamp. Now, it's worth remembering here that we're talking about $2 million new builds to about $5 million new bills. And so to have or to not have custom bunk beds isn't something most families are debating right now. But that's why it's interesting because in the end, this isn't really about the bunk beds at all. Instead, it's about what we want our homes to say about us. And right now, modern luxury homes seem to be saying, "This is a place where people gather." Architecture always tells the story of its time. And right now, the story seems to be build the memories into the floor plan. High-end residential design often acts as a preview. It experiments first and eventually some version of those ideas often filter down into the broader market. So even if you're not contemplating a build with a luxury bonus room and built-in bunk beds, it's still fascinating to watch how the top tier of residential design are redefining what it considers to be aspirational. Thanks so much for joining me today. Don't forget to tune in next week for more Homes Explained.

Keep watching to find out why there's never been a better time to be a landlord. RENTAL INCOME Rental income, as a share of the country's GDP, hit an all-time high during the first quarter of 2017. According to the Labor Department, this was due to the high demand and limited supply of housing. Recent numbers reveal that the average rent for a one-bedroom apartment ranges from $830 to more than $2,000 a month depending on the state or city. Meanwhile, single-family rental homes in major US cities generate around $2.3 trillion annually. At this rate, investing in rental property is as lucrative as investing in the stock market, so the sooner you invest, the better. INVESTING TIPS: MAINTENANCE Of course, owning and managing a rental property come with expenses such as repair and maintenance costs, insurance, property taxes, and homeowner association fees. Landlords tend to spend too much on unnecessary upgrades that will likely cost them more on maintenance and repairs. When it comes to managing a rental property, it is advisable to keep your standards to a minimum. This helps you keep your monthly rent at an appropriate ratio of about twice the monthly cost of the property. INVESTING TIPS: TENANTS One way to take advantage of your rental property is to choose your tenants wisely. Having tenants who do not respect your property or pay their dues on time can cost you valuable time and money that you could have been invested in a more courteous tenant. Screen your tenants carefully and only accept tenants that come with multiple reliable references and don't have a prior history of eviction. It's a landlord's market right now, and signs indicate that that won't change anytime soon. The sooner you invest, the sooner you'll gain your financial independence. Here is my own situation: 
Mortgage: $1189
Comparable Home for rent: $2500 

With the low mortgage (home is very new and fingers crossed no big repair bills but certainly still possible). Lets just say that if I had a $2500 rent, after everything is spent, I would not have anything left to invest in the market. With low mortgage at $1189, compared to $2500 this leaves me $15K to invest every year. Why on earth would I rent at $2500 a month and have zero to invest afterwards? Instead, at the moment I am investing 15% into 401K, not debt and $50-60K every year into the markets. This would be greatly hindered at $2500 not to mention it's going to go up again next year and the year after that forever (yes, more than increases in tax and insurance).

Trump did limit the SALT deduction to only $10k/yr in 2018, but the good news is the limitation is now increased to $40k/yr. Given that tax implications are big factors in the own/rent debate, it may be best to fully understand the tax code (not just IRS, but for your state/local taxes too, where they apply).

to cost to repair any of these issues so that way you're prepared for them and you can ask the seller to drop the selling price by the amount of money it will take to repair the issues some sellers will go for this because they would rather face this than having you potentially back out of the deal if extensive issues are found during the inspection and if the seller is not willing to reduce the price to account for the issues then you may have to back out of the deal and go find another investment property all right guys step number nine is to close escrow closing escrow is the final step to Based on the latest data and reports, there is strong evidence that housing affordability in 2025 is indeed at crisis levels, much higher than in previous decades. Reports highlight that home prices have risen dramatically—home prices relative to median household income are at an all-time high. The median price of a single-family home is now out of reach for roughly 75% of U.S. households. 

Renters also face significant burdens. For the lowest-income renters, there's a severe shortage: only about 35 affordable and available homes exist for every 100 extremely low-income renter households. In addition, rents have grown more quickly than wages over the past few years. Many renters are now paying more than half their income on rent.

Compared to 40 or 50 years ago, the affordability picture has worsened significantly. While rental costs may have seemed stable over longer horizons, the key factor is that home prices have far outpaced income growth, and rental costs have also climbed faster than wages in the last two decades. 

So, while some may argue that renting has remained relatively stable over half a century, the broader housing affordability crisis today—when factoring in homeownership and the overall cost of living—is more acute than in past generations.
