From the early 1980s all the way to 2022, interest rates in the US went basically one direction. In 1981, the 30-year mortgage rate was around 18%. By 2021, it was around 3%. Now, here's why that matters. [music] When interest rates fall, real estate prices go up. It's almost mechanical. Lower rates mean lower monthly payments, which means buyers can afford to pay more for the same house. More buyers chasing the same houses, prices go up. This happened slowly but consistently for four decades straight. [music] So, the people who bought houses in the '80s and '90s, they didn't just benefit from appreciation, they kept refinancing into lower and lower rates. [music] More cash in the present and more equity value in their homes. That's the whole playbook, and it worked incredibly well. Not because real estate is magic, but because interest rates had one of the longest one-way runs in financial history. Here's the problem. That run is over. In the pandemic, we dropped interest rates to zero. We hit the bottom, and we'll have to raise rates before we can take them back down again. So, the same tailwind that quietly made millions of people rich over the last 40 years, it's gone. And most of the books, podcasts, and YouTube videos teaching you to buy real estate were written when that tailwind was in full force. Think about what this means for someone buying their first rental property today. You're not just buying a house, you're making a bet that the prices are going to keep going up without the same interest rate engine pushing them. But the reason everyone wants to own real estate has nothing to do with the actual [music] returns. Real estate is the easiest investment to understand. You can drive past it. You can touch the walls. You know what a house [music] is. Compare that to buying a share of a business or an index fund. That's abstract. You can't see it. There's another psychological trap here, the I know somebody who got rich in real estate effect. You probably know someone exactly like that. Maybe it's a family member, a neighbor, a coworker. Their story sticks with you because real estate success is visible and concrete. You can see the house. You know the price they paid, but you don't hear about the landlord who lost $500,000 on a Florida house that they couldn't sell. That story doesn't get told at dinner parties. When you hear a real estate success story, ask one question the storyteller never mentions. What was the interest rate environment when they bought? Nine times out of 10, they bought during a period of falling or lower rates. The environment did half the work [music] for them. Now, before we get into the winners, the losers, and my playbook for how normal people can build wealth, I want to hear from you. Every week, I'm interviewing normal people who found unique ways to build wealth on my podcast, Boring Money. If you want to talk about how you can compound to the next level, click on the link below and tell me a bit about your business. So, who is making money in real estate in 2026? I'll be honest with you, it's [music] probably not who you think. Let me give you the honest answer. The people making money in real estate in 2026 are institutions, private equity firms, large funds, groups with hundreds of millions of dollars in capital. They bought up single-family homes after 2020 at scale. They own apartment complexes. They have [music] access to debt that regular people can't get. They can accept lower returns because they're playing a volume game, and they've made it extremely hard for the average person [music] to compete. Think about it this way. When a private equity firm bids on a house or a whole neighborhood of houses, they don't need it to cash flow well right now. They have patient capital. They can wait. They can absorb carrying cost. They're playing a different game than you are. [music] Meanwhile, you're trying to make the numbers work on one house at 7% interest rates. [music] You're not competing on the same field. You're not even in the same stadium. Here's where it gets really ugly. Let me show you the math on a real >> [music] >> market. 10 years ago, a median home in Nashville was around $220,000. Let's say you put 20% down, or $44,000. Your mortgage at a 4% rate was roughly $840 a month. Rent in that neighborhood, maybe $1,400 a month. That's $560 a month in positive cash flow before expenses. [music] Today, that same house is $450,000. If you put 20% down, that's $90,000. Your mortgage at 7%, roughly $2,400 [music] a month. Rent in that same neighborhood today, maybe $2,200 a month. You're already underwater before you fix a single broken appliance. Now, [music] that's not a fringe case. That's most markets in America right now. Run this simple test on any property you're considering. Take your total monthly cost, your mortgage, your taxes, your insurance, any HOA fees, and compare it to what you could realistically rent it for. If those numbers are close or rent is lower, walk away. The margin isn't there to absorb the surprises that will definitely come. Even if the cash flow math works, there's a cost in real estate that almost nobody accounts for, and it will quietly destroy your returns. [music] Rental real estate is a business. It's just a business that most people don't treat like one. And like any business, it has operating cost. The water heater breaks, the HVAC system goes out, and as someone who runs an air filter company, I can tell you HVAC replacements aren't cheap. Every one of those events wipes out months of cash flow. I talk to a lot of landlords who will tell me that they're making $400 a month on a rental. Then I ask, "Have you had any major repair in the last 3 years?" And then they say yes. And when I ask what it cost, it's almost always a year's worth of that $400. So, the real return is nothing or close to it. And that's not even including the time that you're spending managing it. Now, add a property manager, because at some point you will want to take some time back, they take 8 to 10% of rent, sometimes more. On a $2,000 a month rental property, that's $200 off the top every [music] month before a single bill gets paid. The cash flow evaporates. I'm not saying this to be harsh, I'm saying this because I see people make this mistake all the time, and the real cost only becomes clear two or three years in. You're a landlord, which means you're a maintenance coordinator. You're a small business owner with thin margins, difficult customers, and assets that depreciate while you sleep. When you're evaluating property, don't just model the cash flow, model your time. How many hours a month will this property take, including calls, repairs, showings, and stress? Then, multiply those hours by what your time is worth. Add that back to your cost. Now, what does the return really look like? If you think commercial real estate is the smarter play, more professional, more stable, better tenants, you need to hear what's actually happening right now. Commercial real estate looked like the safest bet in the world until it didn't. 20 years ago, a Manhattan office building was considered one of the safest assets on earth. Institutional grade, irreplaceable location, blue-chip tenants. Today, some of those buildings are basically empty. Remote work changed everything. Companies downsized their offices. Leases expired and didn't get renewed. And now, you have these massive, expensive buildings that nobody wants. And the cost to convert them into something useful is often more than they're worth. Assets that looked permanent just stopped being valuable. This is the core risk in any real estate investment that most people never take seriously. The world changes in ways we can't imagine. Demand shifts, and real estate is extremely illiquid, which means by the time you see the problem, it's very hard or impossible to get out. The people who owned Manhattan office buildings a decade ago were not amateurs. They were sophisticated investors with access to the best information, the best lawyers, the best advisors, and they still got blindsided. If the smartest money in the room can lose on real estate, the idea that it's a safe place for your savings deserves a lot more scrutiny. If you're seriously considering commercial real estate, ask yourself this. What is the 10-year trend for this type of property? Is demand for this space growing or shrinking? Okay, this isn't all bad. There are actually much better, more flexible opportunities that are opening right now. At the end of this section, I'll show you three ways [music] that I would strategically use some savings. People ask me all the time what I invest in. My answer is pretty boring, and it's working. Here's my actual portfolio, no fluff, no flexing. I'll be straight with you. Almost all my money is in Filterbuy. I am deeply concentrated in one business, and that's intentional. I know it better than anything else. I can control the outcome, and the return on capital is much higher than anything that I could get in real estate. That's the first principle. Put your money where you have the most knowledge and the most control. Building wealth comes from focusing on your unique advantages and skills. [music] Preserving wealth comes from buying a bunch of assets. For the portion of my money that's outside the business, I keep it simple. Broad stock market ETFs, basically owning a tiny piece of thousands of American companies at once, and some Bitcoin. That's it. No rental properties, no commercial real estate, no complex structures. Now, does real estate ever make sense? Yes, absolutely. If you really understand a specific market, and you can find a really good deal, and you're willing to put in in the work, it can absolutely work. I'm not saying real estate is evil. I'm just saying the default assumption that it's the safest path to wealth needs to be challenged hard right now. Here's something Ramit Sethi has talked about that I think is underrated. In many markets right now, it is cheaper to rent than to own. Not because renting is throwing money away, that's a myth, but because the true cost of ownership, when you include maintenance, taxes, insurance, and the opportunity cost of your down payment, is often 30 to 40% higher than renting. In those markets, the person renting and investing the difference is coming out ahead. This goes against everything we were taught. It goes against the gut feeling that owning is always better. But the math doesn't care about your gut feeling. Go look up the price-to-rent ratio in your city right now. You can ask an AI to calculate it easily today. If that number is above 20, renting and investing is almost always the better financial move. In many major cities right now, that number is 25, 30, or even higher. So, if you have cash to deploy, and someone tells you to put it in a rental property, here's what I'd actually do instead. First, and this is the most important one, bet on yourself. If you're young, cash in the bank is not a down payment on a house. It's a runway to take real swings in your career or your business. My favorite move, find a business owner who's thinking about retiring. Offer to work for them for free for a few months to learn how the business runs. Then, negotiate a deal where they finance the sale themselves. That's what's called an owner earnout. Basically, you grow their company, they get a passive cut of profits, and eventually the business is yours. Having money in the bank gives you the flexibility to take a risk like that. Most people can't even consider it because they don't have the cushion. That's the highest upside move that I know. Second, if you're not ready for that, save 15% of your income every year without fail. Put it in a low-cost index fund like the S&P 500 ETF, the total market ETF, something boring that tracks the whole market. Don't overthink it, just do it consistently. Third, invest in your skills. This is the thing that nobody wants to hear, but it's the most true. The skills that make you more valuable in the marketplace compound over time, just like money does. A 25-year-old who spends $10,000 improving their ability to sell, to manage people, or to understand AI will see a return on that investment that dwarfs almost any real estate. The boring truth is that your earning power is your biggest asset right now. Protect and grow that before you try to put money to work anywhere else. People want passive income, and I understand that. But here's the thing about passive income, it's a myth when you're starting out. Every passive investment requires active work to build first. The dividend stock portfolio requires years of saving and investing. The rental property requires capital, time, and management. The business requires building. There's no version where you skip the work and get the results. The question isn't how do I make money without working? The question is, where should I put my work and my money so it compounds the fastest? [music] And right now, for most people, the answer is not a rental property. If you want to see exactly how I think about building and buying businesses instead of real estate, watch this video next. I break down the whole playbook.

- How Real Estate is a Financial Investment | The Real Estate Finance Matrix: Kind: captions Language: en-US Hey, this is Trevor. And if you've ever been puzzled by how real estate and finance are related, keep watching because I'm going to simplify the whole thing for you to make it easy to understand and stay at the end because I'm gonna give you some resources that you can use going forward. A lot of people are terrified of the finance part of investing in real estate because it seems like some unsolvable puzzle, but it's not that complicated. Real estate. Finance is just a matrix and it's actually a pretty simple matrix. So let me show you how it works. Imagine a real estate investor looking at a piece of property, let's say an apartment building that they wanna buy, the investor isn't really looking to just own the property. Why do we invest in real estate to build wealth? So what we're really looking for is the income and that income can come from the cash flow, it can come from appreciation come from the tax benefits. But imagine investing, say like a million dollars and then only making say $100 a year. That's not really what we're looking for, right? It's about building an acceptable level of wealth. And the way to determine whether or not an investment is acceptable is by measuring the return on investment. Real estate investors need to be able to measure how much wealth they are generating relative to the amount that they invested. Let's say we have a million dollar investment. The owner collects the rents and other income. They pay all the expenses like taxes and insurance and utilities and whatever's left over is the net operating income or the noi if we paid all cash for the building, that no oi that's left over after all the bills are paid, that is our cash to keep. So if we take the N oi that we get to take home divided by how much we paid for the building, that's our return on investment also known as the cap rate, but that's only our return on investment. If we paid all cash for the building, most of us don't pay all cash because one of the benefits of real estate is the ability to use leverage and leverage means using part of our own money as equity, part of the bank's money as debt so that we can buy bigger property and scale. And we do that by taking out a mortgage, we bring our own money for the down payment. We borrow the rest from the bank and we use that as our total investment. And this is the financing, this is where real estate finance comes into play. Now, going back to the net operating income, the income from the property, it is what it is. Regardless of how we finance the building, whether we paid all cash or we got part loan or multiple loans, the property still generates a certain amount of net operating income. But whenever we take out a mortgage, a portion of the net operating income is going to go to the lender in the form of loan payments or annual debt service. And then whatever is left over, the investor keeps as net cash flow and the total cash each year going back to the lender, whether it's interest or principal or both when it's divided by the loan amount is called the loan constant. And that's effectively the lenders cash return back to them for the money that they laid out, the more we borrow, the more cash flow we have to give back to the bank. But that's not the only thing that can affect our loan payments. When interest rates go up, we have to borrow less money to keep the payment the same. And when interest rates go up a lot, it really starts to impact the cash flow. The more expensive a loan is the less cash flow is left over for the investor. But as the investor, it's the net cash flow that we want. And the way we measure our return is by taking our net cash flow and dividing it by how much we put in the property that gives us our cash on cash return, our annual return on equity. But that's only the first part of the story because we typically invest over time. And in order to understand return on investment over time, you need to be able to understand the time value of money. Understanding the concept of time value of money is key to knowing your numbers because it's not just what we make in one year, but what we invest in the beginning to acquire the investment, the cash flow positive or negative that we earn along the way, which can vary and then whatever gain or loss we had when we sold the property. And all of this is very easy to calculate if you know how to work a financial calculator. Same thing with calculating loan payments for debt service. This is the road map that you need to take in order to really understand real estate investments. And the first thing of course is you have to know your numbers and that's what we're talking about here, understanding the time value of money, how to calculate loan payments and debt service and how to calculate your returns. My know your numbers crash course is a quick little boot camp on finance analysis and it only takes a few minutes to learn all of this. Once you understand how to run your numbers, then you just need to learn how to analyze investment properties. mortgage loans and leverage to calculate returns because you can't calculate mortgage loans unless you understand things like net operating income and debt service coverage. So they all work together. And for my students, once they know how to analyze properties and leverage, then I teach them how to identify properties that fit their investment strategy. And if you know your numbers, then you can tell if it actually pencils. I think this is where a lot of people get tripped up because they start looking at properties. But they don't actually know if they can get a loan or if they can how much of a loan they can get. Then I teach my students how to negotiate offers, how to do due diligence, how to get financing or raise capital and then of course how to close the deal. unveiled a new Titan in the American Commercial narrative a man by the name of Cornelius Vanderbilt affectionately known as The Commodore his tale opens in the rugged setting of Staten Island New York in 1794 birthed fourth in a lineage of nine young Cornelius was cast in The Forge of humility born into an environment of limited means looking back through history the seeds of the Vanderbilt Legacy can be traced to dilt nestled in the heart of utre Netherlands here Yan eron cornelius's great great great grandfather and a humble Dutch farmer sought a new life in New Amsterdam the city that would eventually bear the name New York in an amazing twist of fate this ancestor of one of America's wealthiest dynasties came to the new world not only peniles but was forced into indentured servitude upon his arrival in 1650 however his backbreaking work would turn out to be the sacrifice that set the Vanderbilt Legacy in motion his village Name suffixed by the Dutch Vander translated to of the built eventually morphed over Generations into the weighty name of Vanderbilt now the Inception of cornelius's empire was marked at the tender age of 16 as he gambled on a ferry service operation between Staten Island and Manhattan funded by a loan of $100 from his mother this modest Venture bloomed into a colossal Fortune exceeding 100 $ million a staggering sum that today's standards would value over $200 billion Cornelius Vanderbilt a figure cast in the mold of determination and intelligence etched his path to Prosperity through an inexhaustible work ethic and shrewd business maneuvering his simple fery service expanded its reach during the tumultuous times of the War of 1812 serving the far-flung government outposts strewn around New York City as peace was restored Vanderbilt shifted his sights to the steamship industry aligning himself with the Strategic Acumen of Thomas Gibbons possessing an innate radar for identifying opportunities within fledgling markets Vanderbilt seized control of the steamboat and nent railroad Industries effectively transforming the American landscape in particular his strategic Mastery lay in his ability to grow markets outmaneuvering competition through offering accessible prices and exploring untapped territories he built his businesses on the burgeoning pillars of customer satisfaction efficiency frugality and uncompromising consequences for dishonesty or idleness and even better this agile Titan was no stranger to Industry Evolution his adaptability became evident when he smoothly transitioned from steamboats to railroads not merely content with business savvy Vanderbilt was also a ruthless competitor Infamous for his aggressive tactics in obliterating competition and with regard to his temperament as a man of few words Vanderbilt was esteemed for his impeccable Jud ment of character he often remained stoic in the face of criticism steadfast in his decisions his unyielding determination and adaptability this drive allowed him to amass a formidable Fortune crowning him as one of the wealthiest Americans of the 19th century in fact his lifestyle could be described as having an even spartan-like frugality a trait inherited from his mother this very deliberate lifestyle Choice marked him as an outlier creating a tangible Rift between himself and the glittering upper echelons of high society yet unbeknownst to the Commodore a dramatic shift loomed on the horizon his descendants heirs to the Vanderbilt Fortune would Cast Away their patriarch's doctrine of frugality they instead embraced a life dripping with extravagance and opulence casting aside the lessons of their forebear and eventually as the kids today would say fumbling the bag you see their actions would signal a stark departure from cornelius's principles setting the stage for a story of of Epic downfall and squandered wealth a taale as Grand as their inheritance filled with missteps excesses and lavish spending that would threaten the very existence of the Vanderbilt Fortune with the Arsenal of family lineage and robust wealth at their disposal one could imagine the Vanderbilts flourishing as an enduring emblem of American old money yet in an unexpected twist of fate Cornelius Vanderbilt's descendants took a nose dive into the Realms of opulence and luxurious ious excess the precursor to this ill-fated Journey was William Henry Vanderbilt cornelius's son despite his pedigree he failed to instill the old money ethos into his eight Offspring arguably raising several who were alas spoiled Bratz beyond measure in his personal life William managed to amass an estate worth $200 million by the time of his demise in 1885 doubling his father's Empire however Whispers of his volatile temperament and emotional incon consistency cast a shadow over his accomplishments and likely hint at how he would come to raise a generation of intemperate progeny early anecdotes recount Tales reminiscent of HBO's succession portraying the relationship between William and his father Cornelius akin to that of Logan Roy and Kendall Roy Cornelius often frustrated with his eldest son called him a blockhead and blatherskite William yearned to prove otherwise but lacked the courage to face the commodore's censure however a turning point arrived during a family Voyage to Europe aboard the steamship Vanderbilt in 1860 following this trip Father and Son grew closer and William assumed an increasingly integral role in the Vanderbilt business Empire yet once at the helm William's unpredictable nature became a public nuisance one incident in particular occurred in 1883 when journalist John Dickinson Sherman questioned him about his operation of The Limited Express train service one to a question about whether the trains were running for public convenience William retorted the public be damned this Outburst reflected the growing Detachment of the Vanderbilt lineage from the principles of their patriarch upon the demise of William Henry Vanderbilt the family's Financial Leviathan passed into the hands of his sons Cornelius Vanderbilt II and William kissum Vanderbilt this heralded a new era in the diny's narrative one marked by an increasingly convoluted distribution of wealth the vast Vanderbilt Fortune once a concentrated reservoir of wealth began to spread thin across an Ever growing family tree with each passing Generation The Inheritance split among a growing number of descendants as family branches multiplied the division of assets exacerbated gradually diluting the individual shares of the once enormous family wealth simultaneously the formidable grasp of inheritance taxes began to gore at the edges of the family's Fortune as one generation passed and handed down their wealth to the next each trans was met with sizable tax implications over time these seemingly harmless nibbles transformed into significant bites out of the family coffers the shadow of such Financial burdens grew longer with each subsequent generation steadily eroding the formidable Vanderbilt Fortune worse still the family was plagued by a series of Reckless Financial choices much of the Vanderbuilt progeny far removed from the industrious ethos of their forebear failed to demonstrate the same business Acumen and financial prudence that Cornelius Vanderbilt had once embodied high-risk Ventures failed Investments and a lack of interest in nurturing the family's business interests dealt a series of devastating blows to their wealth compounding this was their inclination towards a luxurious lifestyle unlike the Commodore The Descendants reveled in extravagance and opulence they spent prodigiously indulging in Grand Mansions ostentatious parties and lavish art collections such unrestrained spending only served to accelerate the decline of the once mighty Vanderbilt Fortune this combination of expanding family size punishing inheritance taxes imprudent financial decisions and unabated spending cast a forboding shadow over the Vanderbilt Dynasty the fortune once a Titan in the annals of American Wealth gradually began its descent into Oblivion by the mid 20th century the Vanderbilt Star had considerably dimmed in the American public sphere as evidenced by the fate of their grand Fifth Avenue Mansions many of which were sold de demolished or repurposed as museums a mere 30 years post Cornelius Vanderbilt's death not a single Vanderbilt was listed among the wealthiest Americans tragically within half a century of the commodore's passing the vast Vanderbilt Fortune had entirely evaporated now let us delve deeper into the distinctive factors that precipitated the decline of the Vanderbilt wealth and juxtapose it with a family that despite amassing wealth during the same era managed to sustain it through generations to understand the contrasts let's first consider a hypothetical scenario imagine a dominant figure at the helm of a multi-billion dollar family Empire suddenly succumbing to illness the CEO who doesn't belong to the family has handpicked a COO as his successor yet the board remains divided in its support the company's Financial Health is waning prompting board anxieties without the board's unanimous backing exploring alternative CEO candidates becomes imperative further convoluting matters is the greenhorn board chair a family member with another family member and controlling shareholder expecting this chair to ascend as the next CEO this scenario may seem unusual but in the world of family-owned businesses finding a successor is a complex Labyrinth the Vanderbilt family story mirrors this scenario the dispersion of their Fortune during the third generation corresponded with diminishing family interest in their key asset the New York Central coupled with escalating expenses the Vanderbilts were known for their philanthropy especially William kissum Vanderbilt of the third generation who donated Millions to numerous causes yet the dawn of income and estate taxes and the family's incessant spending hastened their wealth's decline during the initial stages of their wealth accumulation there was a minimal business regulatory framework allowing them a free hand in dominating entire Industries the era they thrived in was marked by an absence of income and estate taxes which meant that the Vanderbilts were able to amass an unimaginable Fortune free of governmental restrictions or financial drains however as societal norms and governmental policies evolved the Vanderbilts found themselves facing new challenges this era also saw an increased regulatory focus on monopolies and business practices adding another layer of complexity to maintaining and growing their Fortune further exacerbating this situation was the Great Depression which waked havoc on the American economy it affected the Vanderbilt's Investments and economic interests leading to a sign ific downturn in their fortunes the Rockefeller family conversely employed shrewd strategies to safeguard their wealth John D rockefeller's business via generation skipping trusts ensured wealth propagation through the generations a generation skipping trust allows the transfer of substantial amounts of money tax-free skipping a generation usually passing from the gr to the grandchildren rather than the children in the rockefeller's case they managed to retain and multiply their wealth by channeling it down to more distant Generations rather than Distributing it among immediate successors the Vanderbilts however after a few Generations lacked an established Core Business that could continuously feed wealth into their trust their approach primarily involved the passing of assets directly from parent to child in each generation this strategy not only exposed their wealth to the successive imposition of estate taxes with each generational transfer but also made their Fortune vulnerable to the lavish spending habits of each inheritor the Rockefellers by structuring their wealth transfer through a generation skipping trust managed to avoid these pitfalls and ensure the long-term preservation of their fam's wealth and what else set the Rockefellers apart David Rockefeller Jr chairman of Rockefeller and Company in a rare interview spoke of a system of values traditions and institutions developed by the family these mechanisms aided the family in staying United and preserving their wealth serving as practical tools for any family trying to instill sound monetary values in their children remarkably the Rockefeller family largely remained unified devoid of public scandals feuds lawsuits or tragedies that typically plague wealthy dynasties today over 250 direct descendants of John D Rockefeller and Laura Spelman Rockefeller formed the family we meet as a family twice a year often more than 100 of us in a room for a Christmas lunch for example said David Rockefeller from the age of 21 family members are invited to these gatherings aptly called The Family Forum here they discuss the family's Direction new members career news and other significant Milestones they ensure everyone feels part of the family including those who married into it preserving the family history is also deemed essential The Rockefellers do this through their familial homesteads places passed down over Generations where they can reconnect with their past I can go back to the place where my great-grandfather lived over 100 years years ago and see how he lived and see how his son and their grandchildren lived added David Rockefeller such practices exemplify the different approaches two similarly wealthy families took to wealth preservation the Vanderbilt Legacy today is a fascinating tale of resilience adaptation and Lessons Learned From the rise and fall of one of America's wealthiest families despite the family's difficulties and the decline of their once vast Fortune some members of of the Vanderbilt family have managed to create their own paths and leave Lasting Impressions on society Gloria Vanderbilt a descendant of the Vanderbilt family faced a difficult life marked by personal tragedies and challenges born in 1924 she was the daughter of Reginald Claypool Vanderbilt and Gloria Morgan Vanderbilt at a young age Gloria was thrust into the spotlight during a highly publicized custody battle between her mother and her paternal Aunt Gertrude Vanderbilt Whitney despite these early hardships Gloria went on to become a highly respected and successful artist fashion designer and author her resilience and determination to forge her own path endeared her to the public and made her a beloved figure throughout her life Gloria Vanderbilt was not only a colorful but also a very successful personality who made many contributions to the society in which she lived and thrived she was an exceptional vivacious Fashion Icon renowned for her distinctive style and the widely successful Vanderbilt's brand in a fascinating aside one of our followers at Old Money luxury shared a personal interaction with Gloria Vanderbilt recalling I met Gloria Vanderbilt at her New York art show in 2003 she was elegant well-bred polite soft spoken and greeted me with a big smile on her face her hair makeup clothes were impeccable and understated her teeth and complexion were perfect I'm so glad I met her I've read her books she lived appropriately she was an iconic figure who needed protection from Devious people people at her level of wealth she had friendships with designers actors singers lawyers professors business people with education and talent she loved her sons deeply Gloria Sons Anderson Cooper and Carter Vanderbilt Cooper also faced their share of challenges growing up as Vanderbilt descendants Anderson Cooper a well-known journalist and television personality has spoken openly about the impact of his family's history on his life and career He has expressed gratitude for the opportunities his family's Legacy has afforded him while also acknowledging the importance of hard work and self-reliance tragically Carter Vanderbilt Cooper passed by his own hand at the age of 23 a devastating loss for the family The Current financial situation of the Vanderbilt descendants varies as the once enormous family fortune has been significantly diminished over the years many of the family's descendants have pursued careers in various fields from journalism to fashion and have built their own wealth and success independent of the family's Legacy the family's remaining assets and lingering influence can still be seen in the form of their historic Mansions such as the builtmore Estate in Asheville North Carolina and The Breakers in Newport Rhode Island these properties serve as reminders of the family's immense wealth and influence during the Gilded Age and continue to attract visitors from around the world the rise and fall of the Vanderbilts offers valuable lessons for future Generations the importance of responsible wealth management strategic Estate Planning and the potential pitfalls of excessive spending and poor investment decisions are all evident in the family's story Additionally the Vanderbilt Legacy serves as a reminder of the importance of resilience and adaptability in the face of adversity
