- Why Owning a House Feels Impossible Now (The Hidden Housing Crisis): Kind: captions Language: en Imagine doing everything people once believed guaranteed stability. Studying for years, finding steady work, saving carefully, avoiding unnecessary spending, and still realizing that owning a home feels further away than ever. In many cities today, even people with full-time salaries discover that the price of a modest apartment rises faster than their savings account can grow. A person may save for years only to find that by the time they are ready for a down payment, the same property costs significantly more than it did when they started. This creates a strange feeling that many younger workers describe with frustration. They are not only chasing a house, they are chasing a target that keeps moving faster than they can run. What makes this even more confusing is that previous generations often reached home ownership much earlier in life, sometimes on salaries that appear smaller when compared on paper. Yet, across many economies today, buying property has become one of the most difficult financial goals for ordinary households. Housing affordability indicators collected across advanced economies show that in many countries, house prices have increased faster than household incomes over long periods, especially in urban centers where employment opportunities are concentrated. This means that even where wages rise, the ratio between annual income and property prices often becomes less favorable over time. The result is not simply expensive housing. It is a structural shift where ownership increasingly feels delayed, uncertain, or unreachable for large parts of the middle class. At first glance, many people assume this happened because houses became better, cities became more modern, or land simply became scarce. But the deeper explanation is much larger than construction costs alone. The modern housing crisis is tied to interest rates, urban growth, investment behavior, lending systems, and decades of economic policy that changed how property functions inside the economy. To understand why owning a house now feels impossible, we first need to understand that houses are no longer viewed only as places to live. In modern economies, they also became financial assets. And once that happened, the logic of housing changed dramatically. For much of the 20th century, in many countries, home ownership expanded alongside wage growth, suburban development, and relatively affordable land outside growing cities. Governments often supported this through mortgage programs, public infrastructure, and zoning policies that encouraged large-scale residential construction. During those decades, rising incomes and expanding housing supply often moved together strongly enough that many households could realistically expect to buy property after several working years. But over time, something changed. Urban populations grew faster. Economic opportunity became concentrated in fewer major cities. And land near employment centers became increasingly valuable. As more people competed to live near jobs, education, transport, and services, demand intensified in specific areas, while supply often struggled to expand at the same speed. At the same time, mortgage systems became more accessible, which initially helped more people enter the housing market. But this created another effect that many first-time buyers did not notice immediately. When more credit becomes available, more buyers can compete for the same homes, and that often pushes prices higher. Instead of making ownership easier permanently, easier borrowing can increase demand faster than supply, causing property values to rise further. Historical housing data across developed economies shows repeated periods where falling borrowing costs supported stronger house price growth because households could qualify for larger loans than before. This meant monthly affordability sometimes looked manageable even while total property prices climbed rapidly. For buyers, low monthly mortgage rates made expensive houses appear temporarily reachable. But over time, the total debt required became much larger than earlier generations had faced. In many countries, this transformed housing from a gradual family goal into a major long-term financial commitment shaped heavily by debt. Another important shift happened when investors increasingly entered residential property markets. Once housing proved capable of generating both rental income and long-term capital appreciation, property became attractive not only for households, but also for investors seeking stable returns. Apartments, houses, and entire residential blocks increasingly attracted investment capital because they offered something many financial assets could not always guarantee. Physical ownership tied to rising urban demand. This changed market behavior because first-time buyers were no longer competing only with other families. In many places, they also competed with investors who could buy faster, offer more cash, or hold multiple properties. In major urban markets, this effect became especially visible where investment demand strengthened price growth even when wages failed to keep pace. Housing therefore began operating under two different pressures at once. A basic human need for shelter and a financial incentive tied to long-term wealth preservation. That combination made prices more sensitive to financial conditions than many people realized. When interest rates fell for long periods, borrowing became cheaper, investors expanded activity and households increased purchasing power through larger mortgages. But because supply did not always grow equally fast, prices often accelerated further. This is why in many cities, people now feel that even disciplined saving cannot catch up with the market. The problem is not simply that houses cost more. It is that the economic forces behind housing prices often move faster than ordinary household income, especially in locations where opportunity and demand remain concentrated. And once housing becomes tied closely to finance, wages alone are often no longer enough to explain price behavior. The real twist is that for many years, rising house prices were not always treated as a problem. In fact, in many economies, they were often interpreted as a sign of prosperity. When property values increased, homeowners felt wealthier. Banks expanded. Mortgage lending. Construction activity accelerated. And governments often benefited from stronger economic momentum. Rising housing markets created jobs in construction, materials, transport, and legal services, and banking. Entire sectors of the economy began depending on active property markets. This meant that housing stopped being only a social issue and became a major economic engine. But once property prices became closely connected to growth, another hidden pressure appeared. Many institutions quietly developed incentives for prices to remain high rather than fall sharply. Banks earned long-term returns from mortgages. Investors benefited from appreciating assets, and homeowners often supported price stability because much of their personal wealth became tied to property value. This created a system where falling prices could hurt confidence across multiple sectors at once. Historical housing crises show how powerful this connection became. During the global financial crisis of 2007 to 2008, collapsing housing values in several countries did not remain limited to homeowners alone. They spread directly into banking systems. Lending markets and global financial confidence. Mortgage backed financial products lost value. Banks faced major losses and entire economies entered recession. That crisis revealed something important. Housing had become deeply connected to the financial system itself. Since then, many countries have treated housing stability as an economic priority because large disruptions in property markets can quickly affect employment, credit, and national growth. But stabilizing housing markets does not automatically restore affordability. In many places, prices recovered faster than incomes after the crisis, especially once central banks lowered interest rates to support economic recovery. Lower interest rates made mortgages cheaper again, which increased demand, but supply constraints remained in many urban areas. As a result, prices rose again, sometimes even faster than before. This is one reason younger buyers often feel they entered the market at the wrong time. By the time wages improved after economic slowdowns, house prices had often already moved ahead again. Another powerful factor behind the housing crisis is land itself. Contrary to what many assume, construction costs alone do not explain why urban housing becomes expensive. In high demand cities, the most valuable component is often land near economic opportunity. Land near major employment centers, transport systems, universities, and commercial districts becomes limited because location cannot be expanded the way production can. A city can build taller buildings, but it cannot create unlimited central land where millions of people want to live. This means location value rises when economic opportunity concentrates geographically. As technology and services increasingly cluster around major cities, housing demand intensifies exactly where supply faces the strongest limits. Zoning policies then add another layer. In many cities, construction rules, height limits, approval delays, and local planning restrictions slow the speed at which new housing enters the market. Even when demand rises strongly, new supply often arrives too slowly to reduce pressure meaningfully. Economists repeatedly note that where housing supply remains restricted while population and jobs grow, prices tend to rise persistently. This explains why two cities with similar incomes can experience very different affordability outcomes depending on how quickly new housing can legally and physically be added. Mortgage systems also change the psychology of pricing. Buyers often no longer think first about total property price. They think about monthly affordability. If lower interest rates reduce monthly payments, buyers can qualify for larger loans, which allows sellers to ask higher prices because demand expands. Over time, this means lower borrowing costs can raise prices instead of making ownership permanently easier. A family may still manage monthly payments, but only by accepting far larger debt than previous generations carried. This creates the feeling that ownership exists, but only through decades of financial commitment. In some countries, mortgage periods expanded further, stretching repayment horizons across much larger portions of working life. This means ownership remains possible, but increasingly tied to long-term debt exposure that earlier generations often reached with lower price to income ratios. At the same time, wages rarely move with the same speed as housing finance. Salaries usually rise gradually, while borrowing capacity can change quickly when rates move. This is why house prices often react faster than incomes whenever financial conditions shift. A small reduction in mortgage rates can immediately increase what thousands of buyers can bid. But wage growth takes years to create similar purchasing power. Investors understand this dynamic well, which is one reason property remains attractive during low rate environments. Rental demand adds further support because even when households cannot buy, they still need housing, making rental income relatively stable in many cities. This means property often attracts capital even when affordability for first-time buyers weakens. The result is a market where prices respond not only to family demand but also to financial strategy, credit conditions, and long-term expectations. Once these forces combine, the housing market no longer behaves like an ordinary consumer market. It becomes a place where shelter, debt, investment, policy, and long-term wealth all interact at the same time, often pushing ownership further away from the people trying hardest to enter it. Another reason the housing crisis feels so personal is that property prices do not rise in isolation. They rise while other essential costs are also increasing at the same time, which makes the gap between income and ownership feel even larger. A household saving for a down payment is not only competing with house price growth, but also with rent, food, transportation, energy costs, and sometimes student debt or family responsibilities. This means the ability to save is shaped by every other pressure in the economy. before housing even enters the final decision. In many major cities, rent itself has become one of the biggest barriers to ownership because people trying to save for a future home are often paying a large portion of their monthly salary simply to remain close to work. When rent absorbs a significant share of income, savings grow slowly and slow savings become a serious disadvantage in markets where prices move quickly. This creates a difficult cycle. Households rent because buying is expensive, but renting itself reduces the speed at which buying becomes possible. Over time, many first-time buyers discover that the market has moved further while they were still trying to save enough to enter it. This is one reason younger generations often describe home ownership not as delayed, but as constantly postponed by forces they cannot fully control. Urban concentration strengthens this problem further because modern economies increasingly reward geographic proximity to opportunity. High-paying jobs, advanced education, specialized healthcare, business networks, and major transport systems are often concentrated in limited metropolitan areas. As more people move toward those centers, housing demand intensifies exactly where land is already scarce. Smaller cities may remain cheaper, but they do not always offer the same income opportunities, which means households often face a trade-off between affordable housing and economic opportunity. For many workers, the higher salary available in large cities is partly canceled by higher housing costs, leaving little real advantage after rent or mortgage payments. This is why even income growth in strong urban labor markets does not automatically solve affordability. A salary may be higher in a major city, but so are the property prices competing for the same limited space. Another major factor is how expectations influence markets. Housing is unusual because buyers do not only react current prices, they react to what they believe prices will do next. If people believe houses will become even more expensive next year, many rush to buy earlier, which itself increases present demand and pushes prices higher. Investors act similarly. If property is expected to remain scarce and valuable, investors often continue buying even when prices already look high because they believe future gains will justify current costs. This expectation-driven behavior creates momentum that can continue for years, especially when supported by strong lending conditions. In many markets, people no longer ask whether property is cheap. They ask whether waiting will make it even less affordable. That belief alone can keep demand strong even when affordability weakens. Central banks indirectly influence this entire cycle through interest rate policy. When inflation rises, central banks increase rates to cool borrowing and slow price pressure across the economy. Higher rates make mortgages more expensive, which can reduce demand and slow housing markets. But this effect creates a new difficulty for buyers. Even if property prices stabilize or decline slightly, the cost of financing becomes higher, which means monthly affordability may still remain difficult. In practical terms, a cheaper house financed at a much higher interest rate can still produce monthly payments that feel unaffordable. This is why many first-time buyers feel trapped between two unfavorable conditions. When rates are low, prices rise quickly. When rates are high, borrowing becomes expensive. In both cases, ownership remains difficult. Historical mortgage data repeatedly shows that housing affordability depends not only on price levels, but also on financing conditions. These two variables rarely improve perfectly at the same time. Another hidden pressure comes from how housing increasingly functions as wealth storage across generations. Families that already own property often accumulate wealth because their homes appreciate over time, while families without property must save from income alone while prices continue rising. This creates widening differences between households that enter ownership early and those that remain outside longer. In many cities, parental support has become a major factor for firsttime buyers because inheritance, family savings, or help with down payments can determine whether entry becomes possible. For households without that support, wages alone often feel too slow relative to market movement. This creates the impression that ownership is no longer determined only by work, but also by timing and existing wealth. This leads to a larger realization. Housing has gradually become one of the clearest examples of how asset growth can move faster than earned income. A person can work harder, earn more, and still struggle to match the pace at which property values increase because the market responds to finance, land scarcity, investor expectations, and policy decisions far beyond any individual salary. Once that becomes clear, the housing crisis looks less like a simple affordability problem and more like a system where multiple economic forces are pushing against first-time ownership at the same time. And this is where the deeper truth behind the housing crisis finally becomes clear. Owning a house feels impossible today. Not because one single part of the economy failed, but because housing now sits at the intersection of nearly every major economic force at once. Wages influence affordability, but so do interest rates, urban planning, global investment flows, land scarcity, construction speed, demographic pressure, and long-term policy decisions made over decades. A buyer entering the market today is not simply purchasing shelter. They are entering a system shaped by credit conditions, financial expectations, and the accumulated effects of years in which housing increasingly behaved like an asset before it behaved like a basic need. This explains why many people feel confused when they compare their situation with previous generations. Earlier generations often bought property in environments where house price to income ratios were lower, debt periods shorter, and financial competition weaker. In many cases, housing demand existed, but investment pressure, global capital movement, and urban concentration had not yet reached the same intensity. Today, a first-time buyer often competes inside a far more complex market where property attracts not only families, but also investors, long-term wealth strategies, and financial institutions that view housing as part of broader capital allocation. This does not mean ownership has become impossible everywhere, but it does mean the path toward ownership is no longer shaped by salary alone. In many countries, households now require larger down payments, longer mortgage commitments, and far more patience than earlier generations needed. Even when wages improve, the size of debt required often changes how ownership feels psychologically. A person may technically qualify for a mortgage, yet still hesitate because repayment stretches across decades, and uncertainty about future rates, job stability, or inflation remains high. This psychological pressure is one reason housing feels harder even when financing exists. Ownership no longer represents only achievement. For many people, it also represents long-term financial risk. Governments understand this tension and have tried different responses. From first-time buyer support to social housing expansion, zoning reform, and mortgage regulation. Some policies help temporarily by increasing supply, lowering entry barriers, or slowing speculative demand. But no single policy fully reverses decades of accumulated price pressure quickly because the underlying forces remain powerful. If cities continue concentrating opportunity while supply remains limited, demand pressure returns. If rates fall sharply, borrowing expands again. If investment capital seeks stable assets, property often remains attractive. This is why many housing reforms improve conditions only gradually rather than immediately transforming affordability. Another important realization is that housing markets do not move uniformly. Some regions cool while others remain expensive. Some cities expand supply more effectively than others, and some countries manage affordability better because policy reacts earlier to demand pressure. But globally, the same pattern appears repeatedly, where economic opportunity concentrates faster than housing supply expands. Prices rise beyond wage growth over long periods. That pattern explains why housing frustration now appears in so many different countries at once. It is not a local accident, but a repeated economic outcome in modern urban systems. And perhaps the most important perspective shift is this. The housing crisis is not only about expensive homes. It is about time. People increasingly feel that ownership requires more years of work, more years of saving, more years of waiting, and more years of financial discipline than before. A goal that once felt reachable after several working years can now feel postponed deep into adulthood, even for people with stable employment. This changes how entire generations think about family planning, mobility, long-term security, and even career decisions. When ownership moves further away, housing stops being only a market issue and becomes a social one because it affects how people imagine stability itself. The next time someone says owning a house feels impossible, they may not simply be reacting to one expensive property listing. They may be reacting to an entire economic system where prices respond faster than wages, where land near opportunity remains limited, where debt determines access, and where housing carries financial meaning far beyond shelter. Once that becomes visible, the modern housing crisis stops looking like a mystery and starts looking like the natural result of many powerful forces moving together for many years. And that is why in so many cities around the world today, a house can feel less like something people buy when life becomes stable and more like something stability itself now depends on reaching.

beyond the bricks and mortar offering a unique blend of comfort convenience and exclusivity High net worth individuals have a unique perspective on wealth preservation and growth they don't just focus on accumulating wealth they are strategic about how they manage and grow it they understand the importance of diversification and seek Investments that offer both stability and long-term value appreciation diversification is not just a buzzword for them it's a fundamental principle that guides their investment decision this is where real estate particularly luxxury real estate comes into play luxury real estate is not just about owning a beautiful home it's about making a Strategic investment that can yield significant returns unlike stocks or bonds which can be volatile and susceptible to Market fluctuations real estate represents a tangible asset this tangibility provides a sense of security that other Investments often lack it's a physical entity that provides a sense of security and permanence the value of a well-located well-maintained property is unlikely to diminish drastically making it a safer bet for long-term investment this inherent stability makes it an attractive investment option for those seeking to safeguard their wealth real estate Investments can also provide a steady income stream through rental yields adding another layer of Financial Security moreover luxury real estate often appreciates in value over time the demand for Prime real estate in desirable locations tends to increase driving up property values Prime locations remain desirable and well-maintained luxury properties become even more exclusive as they age this exclusivity can lead to significant capital appreciation making luxury real estate a lucrative investment wildly currencies can devalue and even bonds can lose their luster in times of turbulence investors seek assets that can weather the storm providing stability and preserving their wealth they look for Investments that are less susceptible to the whims of the market this is where the concept of Safe Haven investing comes into play safe hav assets are those that investors flock to during periods of Market stress Safe Haven assets are those that are expected to retain or increase in value during times of Market stress traditionally these have included gold government bonds and certain currencies they act as a hedge against risk offering investors a sense of security when other asset classes are faltering but in recent years another asset class has emerged as a preferred Safe Haven luxury real estate luxury real estate with its inherent stability and intrinsic value has emerged as a preferred Safe Haven asset for Discerning investors its appeal lies in its tangible nature and the prestige associated with owning high-end properties during economic downturns while stock markets May plummet and currencies fluctuate luxury real estate tends to hold its value this is because the demand for Prime real estate remains strong even in challenging times this resilience stems from several factors including its limited Supply there are only so many prime locations and high-end properties Well, taking a step back, if I may, um I think that what we're living with is a consequence of um of two big trends. One of them is something that started in the 1920s and I think we've we've maybe even talked about this before when uh Herbert Hoover as Secretary of Commerce kind of popularized the way we do planning right now which is to it's all centered around turning farms into subdivisions. And the financial model of that is you have a developer come along and he and he or she says I'm going to turn this farm into a whole community. You kind of like happened in Irvine for example. And part of that is paying for the infrastructure, paying for the roads, and paying for the schools and paying for all the things. And the developer would usually take responsibility for all of that stuff because there's really nothing there. And the upside of transforming agricultural land into residential land could be enormous. I mean, four, five, 6, 7,000% upside if you do something like that. which is why it can make sense if you're turning a farm into a subdivision to have that sort of a model where the developer takes responsibility for everything in that sort of suburban development paradigm. There's nothing there before the developer and there's a lot of margin because you guys got farmland and you're turning into housing and you make a lot of money. So pennies on the dollar. Exactly. Meanwhile, those of us who are in the infill world, which means builders who are building in existing cities and often much more incremental projects, 50 units, 100 units, something like that, it doesn't make sense to have us build the roads and the schools and the sewers. But that's still the structure of how the city regulates. And so, um, that culture of having the developer, uh, do everything is still very, very much part of the planning and engineering culture of most cities, including Los Angeles. So, so when I show up, I'm expected to take care of all of that stuff. And when you drive through LA and you see, you know, an old uh kind of 19 uh 1930s street with beautiful buildings on it and then there's a new apartment building and the street widens suddenly for just the width of that apartment building. That's what you're seeing. You're seeing a city that says the developer must build the streets and so they make you build, you know, 30 feet of a street, which is ridiculous, but that's that's what happens. The second issue with the city is that there's a great sense of disempowerment among public officials where um they don't I I think it's something that's happened in our civic culture over the past 50 years where we people in government don't feel like they have the tools to directly do very much anymore and they see their primary power as being able to find a private actor, a developer or a business person or somebody who can do what needs to get done. So instead of the city um building public housing um they say let's create a system where private developers can build public housing for the city or um instead of the city saying let's build the roads that we want they wait for developers to come along and say okay you you build us 30 ft of that road that we want and so you end up with this really kind of disjointed public realm uh and public safety net frankly because it's crafted bit by bit opportunistically when a developer shows up. And so there is way too much attention being paid to individual projects to kind of solve all of the problems of our city when what really should happen is the city uh which has, you know, tons of budget money and tons of power could be doing a lot more itself. and developers could really just become builders who build the housing that we need and aren't expected to solve our school problem, our road problem, our our everything problem. Now, based on what you're seeing, you've done extensive studies of measure ULA and the mansion tax and and you've seen the transactions are going down. What do you recommend? What should the city do? The um in our report, the unintended consequences of of measure ULA, we actually made a few recommendations. Um one of them would be to turn measure ULA into uh actually what it was sold as. It was sold as a mansion tax and we could make it actually a mansion tax and not tax apartments, not tax commercial centers, industrial centers, etc. that would preserve about 60% of the revenue to measure ULA and uh turn the real estate market back on for these very important components of the market. Another thing that we recommend is to make ULA marginal. I I I hinted at this earlier in the conversation but didn't go into detail. It kicks in abruptly when you cross the threshold. So when it first came into effect, a $5 million transaction was not subject to the tax at all. a $5 million1 transaction is subject to a 4% tax entirely. So suddenly with $1 of additional value, you've got $200,000 of additional cost. And that creates a lot of very strange behavior. Income taxes are marginal, as we say. So if you go into a new tax bracket because you get a raise, your whole salary is isn't taxed at that new level. It's just the portion in that new bracket that's taxed at the level. We think measure ULA should be marginal. So only the the amount over the threshold is taxed at the tax rate. We also recommend short of those two things that we create a waiver for new construction projects. So if you build a new commercial building, if you build a new multif family building, the first 15 years after it's put in service, you should be exempt from the transfer tax. that way. Uh the way most building happens where a builder builds and then sells to an owner and then that owner maybe after 10 years or so will sell to another owner who's going to renovate it again. Um that 15 years should be enough in many of those cases to preserve the economics of those projects and not dissuade people from investing in Los Angeles. [Music] Does the politics of LA get in the middle of all of this? It really does because privately many many people across the political spectrum are acknowledging that Measure ULA uh isn't working as they hoped it would. And so, um, they would like to change it. You know, the the the union workers aren't working. The affordable housing people are getting pummeled by some strange financing requirements in Measure ULA. The market rate builders of housing and commercial aren't able to work anymore. And so, everybody knows that it's not working. But politically, it hasn't become safe to say that publicly yet. And I'm hoping that the Overton window is moving a little bit and people will be able to speak out a little bit more because at the state level, Assembly Member Buffy Wixs from uh from uh Berkeley has put forward something called AB698. It's a what we call a study bill which would require future transfer taxes to do economic analyses before they're before they're put before voters. and uh that bill which made it out of the assembly unanimously uh recently could be amended to make some of the important fixes we need in measure ULA and I'm hoping that as it becomes safer to discuss its shortcomings um that we can craft some solutions that will preserve what's good about it which is to the extent that it can generate hundreds of millions of dollars a year to pay for low-income households to uh have more stable housing and even create more housing for them. Uh we should do that and I'd like to see AB698 be a vehicle for that. So measure ULA was put forward when it was because of a couple of recent court cases which essentially poked a hole in Proposition 13. Proposition 13 says that a special tax and measure ULA is a special tax needs a twothirds vote of the people. These two court cases uh basically said that that is only the case. You only need a two-thirds vote of the people if the special tax is put before voters by a legislative body either a city council or the state legislature or something like this. But if the uh people proposing the tax are the people uh this is what the court case has determined then it only needs a 51% threshold. And so, uh, the backers of Measure ULA kind of raced to get it on the ballot before that loophole in Proposition 13 was closed. It's still open, by the way. And the result of that is, you know, if if the legislature had proposed this transfer tax or if the city council had proposed this transfer tax, it would have gone through countless committees, um, economic analyses. it would have been studied and and people, you know, from the builder community, the banking community would have had a chance to look at it and say, you know, I think it's a good idea, but change paragraph number seven. Instead, what happened is version 1.0 of this very complex measure, a 29page measure that voters were asked to vote yes or no on um made made it made it into law. And it uh it contains lots of idiosyncrasies that had the backers spoken with bankers, had they spoken with builders, could have been fixed. Um things like uh provisions in Measure ULA that make the money incompatible with Fanny May and Freddy Mack loans. So it's become nearly impossible to place it in service and development projects because it's incompatible with the financing those affordable housing projects have. Um, it has resale restrictions that uh prevent projects that got ULA money from uh being able to sell to anybody but a nonprofit. And the the number of nonprofit buyers in the market is tiny compared to the number of regular for-profit companies that buy and sell affordable housing. So, that's created massive problems. Um, you know, we we believe that the changes that we're proposing, which would be um, you know, the 15-year waiver for new construction, uh, if bundled with things like fixing some of the financing provisions of Measure ULA, would preserve the vast majority of the revenues, it would preserve 87% of the revenues, and it would actually make measure ULA much, much easier to put on the street and benefit people. developers somehow don't have the best reputation. Yeah. Can you tell us about that? Sure. Um well, so it's actually interesting. So an organization that I am a part of called the Council of Infill Builders, we chose our name builders very intentionally because that is what we do. We create physical places and cities for people to live and do business and um things like that. development has this reputation of somehow gaming the system of somehow um finding a backdoor um you know to do something that other people aren't allowed to do uh to change the zoning to change the plans and all of this and I think a lot of people perceive development as that as kind of breaking a community's idea of itself and gentrifying areas gentrifying, changing the character. Um there there's an exploitive um there's an exploitive veilance that some people assign to the word developer, which I don't think is often deserved. But this is why I like the word builder because uh every house that we live in was built by a builder, often by a developer. Every apartment building a developer. And so I think the reputation of development uh is undeserved. Um but it is it's real. It's there. People have that perception. And I think we need to change it. We need people to start thinking of those of us who are creating physical you know the physical spaces where people can live and work and play and uh and do business. Um we need to think of them as as the builders which is what we are. Now, what's the value you guys bring to the table for the community? Building is a skill and it's sort of like um producing a movie. You know, you need to know how to be able to finance stuff. You need to know how to get government approvals to do things the right way. You need to know how to hire the right team of designers and contractors and others. And you also need to have a vision for what you're doing. Um, you know, we all know that the world is changing and we all know that the world needs to change that, you know, when you've got more people in Los Angeles than we have homes, for example, somebody needs to come in and and and find solutions for this and developers/builders are people who have kind of self selected to be the the protagonists in change. I mean, we're it's our job to say, you know, something needs to happen and I'm going to do it. I I I want to create a space for more small businesses on this main street or I want to create a space where more families can live. And um a lot of people will go to planning meetings and talk about zoning changes and stuff like this which is which is good and fine but no zone no zoning ordinance no urban plan has ever actually created anything. Um, in order to create, you need to go out there and get your hands dirty and deal with all the moral complexity of it because it is a morally complex thing when you're introducing change into a neighborhood. And builders are people who said, "I'm willing to bear that burden and I'm willing to do it." And that's that's who we are. Matt Smith, professor at USC and developer. It was great to have you in California insider. So great to be here. Thanks, Cac. Let us know what your thoughts are on this mansion tax. - Top 25 Modern Minimalist House Designs with a Luxurious Touch: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] [Applause] Minimalism is no longer just a trend. It's a lifestyle. But when paired with luxurious materials, thoughtful lighting, and open design concepts, it transforms into something truly timeless. In this video, we'll walk you through the top 25 modern minimalist house designs that embrace simplicity with a high-end twist, showcasing exteriors and interiors that blend tranquility and elegance. A concrete facade offers a strong geometric statement. Clean lines and sharp edges exude modernity while polished finishes or board form textures add depth. The luxury comes from how concrete interacts with sunlight, shadow, and the surrounding landscape, making it a work of art that's both robust and refined. Heat. Heat. That's heat. Flat roofs are a hallmark of modern minimalist homes. Their simple profile allows for seamless integration with the sky, solar panels, or rooftop terraces. A luxurious touch often comes in the form of large overhangs, inset lighting, or elegant parapits, giving the home a sharp, streamlined look. Monochromatic exteriors, particularly matte black against stark white, create a dramatic high fashion effect. The minimalist approach focuses on silhouette and form, while luxury reveals itself in the quality of materials, smooth stucco, brushed metal, and large frameless windows that reflect the environment. Natural wood cladding brings warmth to homes. Vertical or horizontal planks when left raw or lightly stained provide contrast to otherwise cold concrete or steel. The luxury element lies in the quality of the timber. Think cedar teak or IP paired with hidden fastenings and custom joints. Minimalist homes often blur boundaries between indoor and outdoor. entire walls made of sliding glass open spaces to gardens, pools, or courtyards. When paired with automated shades or smart tinting technology, this feature becomes a luxurious futuristic experience of openness and control. Nothing says modern luxury like a minimalist house that floats. Cantalvered upper floors or stairways create visual tension and showcase engineering prowess. These elements, often clad in stone or metal, offer both functional outdoor space beneath and striking architectural statements above. Minimalist homes often hide entryways behind serene garden walls or internal courtyards. These secluded outdoor spaces set the tone. Calm, peaceful, intentional. Luxury emerges in how these spaces connect. Kitchens merge into dining and living areas effortlessly with furnishings and lighting providing subtle spatial definition. Polished concrete floors are not only minimal but also luxurious when executed with precision. Smooth reflective finishes bounce natural light around the space. Alternatively, high-end epoxy coatings and neutral tones offer a seamless high gloss foundation that's both durable and elegant. Fireplaces in minimalist homes become artwork. Think long horizontal gas lines embedded in raw stone walls or sleek black boxes recessed into concrete. These elements add warmth without ornamentation. And their luxury lies in clean technology, custom design, and ambient lighting. Heat. Heat. N. Minimalist kitchens strip away upper cabinets and unnecessary hardware. Built-in storage that vanishes into the wall keeps minimalist interiors pristine. From motorized kitchen cupboards to walk-in closets hidden behind flush panels, luxury comes from the smooth, intuitive experience of opening, closing, and organizing without visible effort. Minimalist interiors often stay within a restrained color palette. whites, beiges, grays, or earthy tones. Lighting becomes a central feature in homes. Think oversized pendant lamps, LED strips hidden in recesses, or custommade chandeliers. These aren't just sources of light. They're floating sculptures that add elegance and mood to every room. Heat. Hey, Heat. Heat. Heat. Minimalist bathrooms are serene spaces with clean lines and tactile surfaces. Luxury enters with heated floors, rainfall showers, freestanding stone tubs, and ambient light. Mirrors are seamless, sinks are integrated, and the feeling is always peaceful, spacious, and pure. Frameless glass is a minimalist signature, especially indoors and windows. Removing visual borders creates uninterrupted views and a seamless transition between indoors and out. When crafted with precision and paired with smart blinds, they become a hallmark of high-end modernism. Bedrooms follow the minimalist mantra uncluttered and calm. The transition from interior to exterior is seamless, often using matching floors or sliding doors. True modern luxury lies in sustainability. warm and calming vibe with the linear strips and framed carpentry design features and organic elements looking subtle yet creating interesting hints of pleasant detailing the Muji aesthetic is based monotonous and empty the modern malay house is not about its aesthetic of what depicts the traditional malay house it is the space planning concept behind the narrative of the architecture the spatial choreography references what is commonly found in a malay kampong house where non-family members may only access as far as the serambi which is addressed as the foyer further spatial hierarchy and space implementation like the rumah dapur the kitchen and rumah ibu, the main house defines how the malay home is resided a Modern Malay house it is about taking the malay spirit of a traditional Malay home and implementing it into a modern contemporary home bringing industrial design to a whole new level completed in greater part of the cement finish a bold concrete slide invites you as a statement the moment you enter exposed roof thrust, wirings and cement rendered walls and floors paired with steel works in black powder coated finish that reflects good contrast against the cement background this home is literally a concrete jungle a home is a symbol of not just an aesthetic style but a statement of family values and lifestyle expectation design trends can be rather subjective my advice would be to build a home that suits your lifestyle and compliments your way of living however, with the right budget to spend for the renovation and certainly thoughtful space planning & good idea development it isnt that strenous to achieve your dream home if you would like to watch any of the above top 10 videos you could refer to this videos description column and you will find all - Florida Fall Home Maintenance Tips | Easy Seasonal Checklist for Homeowners: Kind: captions Language: en Hey friends, fall is here in Florida and while we don't have to worry about raking leaves or shoveling snow like they do up north, there are a few things you'll want to do around your home this season. First up, storm prep. Hurricane season isn't over until the end of November. So, make sure your gutters are clear, your roof looks solid, and make sure those big branches are trimmed back. Trust me, it's way easier to deal with now than after a storm. Next, let's talk about moisture and humidity. Florida stays humid even in the fall, so check your windows and doors for any gaps. That keeps water and bugs out. Bonus, it also helps your AC work more efficiently. Speaking of bugs, this is Florida. The pests never take a vacation. Keep an eye out for ants, termites, and roaches. And make sure you've sealed up cracks around your home with cocking. And don't forget your yard. Adjust your sprinklers since your lawn doesn't need as much water in cooler months. And give your grass a fall fertilizer to keep it healthy. Last tip, safety check. Test your smoke detectors, carbon monoxide detectors, and even your garage door opener. Little things like that make a big difference in the long run. So, while everyone else is sipping pumpkin spice and raking leads, our Florida fall maintenance is all about storm prep, humidity control, and keeping pests out. Do these simple steps and your home will thank you. And hey, if you need a trusted vendor for any of these services, reach out to me. offset the balance of the fan or it can just send dust flying in all directions when you turn it on my wife has a good technique to help clean this she takes an old pillowcase and she wraps it over debris that could build up on this cover right here or on the filter those need to be cleaned on a regular basis as well as well as the hose that leads to the outside now if by chance you have a And with that capacity, uh, 21 kilwatt hours, we can actually run our refrigerator for 14 days during a power outage, which keeps all of our food fresh. And then we also wanted to make sure that we added a large living room window so we get the sun rises in the morning and then the sun sets and it we get all that beautiful light in our home. So we really wanted to make sure that we had a wood stove in our house because we live in Washington so we get a lot of cold evenings. So we installed one of these um dwarf wood stoves since the tiny homes are so insulated. It really does heat up our home so well which is really nice in the winter. Over here we have our bookshelf. One of the hardest things for me about downsizing was actually our books. My family are big readers and we are also big book collectors. So I did have to downsize my books, but I really wanted to make sure we had a space in our home that was able to keep our favorite books and books that we're reading. And so we have our bookshelf here. We also have some of our little plants and pictures and things that just make it feel more at home. and also a picture of my very first tiny house that I lived in that my sister painted for me. We used to live in a 1300 foot house in Bellingham and it cost us about 2400 a month for our mortgage and then our utility bills were anywhere from $300 to $400 a month and now we spend about $700 a month on our land loan which is what we have to live here on our 5 acres and our utilities are minimal compared to our um prior living. So that's allowed so much more freedom with our finances and our time. >> Once the decision was made that we wanted to move away from town, the reality was we couldn't afford land with a house on it. >> And so we bought what we could afford, which was raw land that we felt like we could develop. And then we did what we could afford to do on that land, which was build a tiny house. >> So we built it ourselves from our own design that we the initial drawing was on the napkin. The kids actually built the design several times out of popsicle sticks and hot glue that really helped them get excited about it and visualize the space. >> So, we both have construction background, so it was not a big deal for us to build our tiny house, but we wanted to make sure that we were able to design in a way that would make it feel sustainable for all of us to live. At this point, we had no intention on building tiny homes for a living. We knew we wanted to live in one, but that wasn't a plan for us to do this as a business. But as we were building, we realized how excited we were about like creating a space that was functional and that was beautiful and that was sustainable for not just a couple, like a family could live in it. So yeah, we got a flatbed trailer and started building. >> Actually, it took us a while to build. Not because the building took a while, but we just had other life circumstances. And so we would regularly we would come here for like an hour at a time or maybe 2 hours at a time. the amount of time that we worked on it, I would say it was only like four or five months to build it, >> but we spread it out over a year and a half, maybe. Does that sound right? >> That sounds right. Yeah. >> But now in our business, it only takes us >> three or four months. So that's been a >> Yeah. >> successful change. >> Our business is called Ocean Designs and we focus on tiny living spaces. We started our business and it's been just over a year that we've had the business. And there is just no way that we could have done that full-time and taken that huge risk to start a business without being in our tiny house. And the reason is mostly financial. Like we just to start a business and have a mortgage and have monthly utility payments that are high, we just wouldn't have been able to afford it. And that wouldn't have been possible, I don't think, without being able to live in a tiny house on this property. >> Finding land to put your tiny home on is tricky for pretty much everyone. We were really fortunate that we have a great realtor, but we were also able to find this piece of land in a beautiful valley and we had been kind of searching for a spot to invest in and to live. And this particular location allowed us to do all those things in one spot. And so that's how we found our property was just continuous search. But we actually get this question all the time through our business. And there are a number of people who own property who have RV pads or have other location to place a tiny home. And so we're like try to keep track of that in our local area so that if people have that question and they want to build buy a tiny home or build a tiny home but don't know where to put it, we can connect those two groups up because there's actually quite a few people that have room and >> yeah, >> should have a tiny house on it. >> The reason that we put the bathroom at the end of the tiny house is because our tiny house has two lofts, one for each kid. And so this side made sense since we had our first floor bedroom at one end. We would have the bathroom at one end so that each end could have a loft over top of it. So Morgan's loft is actually right above us. Different from our bedroom, this ceiling height is slightly higher than our bedroom and Morgan's ceiling is slightly lower. And as you may have seen, Merritt is quite tall. So we wanted to make the bathroom easy access for him and his loft easier access for him. In the bathroom, we also have a full-size laundry setup. So, a washer and a separate dryer. They're all electric. So, that just kept with our theme of wanting the entire home to be electric because we have a family of four here. Being able to do laundry on a daily basis is extremely important to us. Uh we also live on 5 acres and we are regularly bringing in dirt and getting dirty. We mountain bike, dirt bike, climb, ski, all those things. And those all require some sort of laundry to be done at the end of of that activity. This tiny house has a full regular flush toilet. And for us, because we own our 5 acres, uh we wanted to install a septic system to manage all of our waste. And that is for future improvement as well. But for our tiny house, that worked out so well cuz we could just use a traditional flush toilet. We wanted a fulls size shower in our tiny house. And so this one is 48 in wide by 34 in deep. And we use a lowflow shower head on it to help us manage water. Even though we're on a well, we just feel like conserving water is important, and we're able to do that partially because of that lowflow uh shower head. I would say though, the bathroom is actually a little too big. If I was going to change anything in here, I would probably shrink the bathroom down a little bit. It just didn't quite need to be as big as we thought it needed to be. Also, in the corner here, we have our electrical panel and some storage. And that was all part of our original design. But one of the things because we went all electric, we needed a 100 amp panel where as a more typical tiny house would be 50 or 30 amps. So ours is quite big. We are hardwired to the grid on that. So we can supply that 100 amps um without any problem. But that allows us to do electric hot water heater as well as all the other electric appliances without having to choose one or the other. From the other side of the house to our bathroom is our bedroom. It is an 8 by10 bedroom which we have put in a full-size bed. You can fit a queen, but we had a fulls size bed, so that's what we used. In our bedroom, we wanted to make sure that we each had our own closet space. So, on either side of the beds, we have these two wardrobes. And then under the bed, we also have a couple of storage bins that we keep more of our photo albums and things like that in. Each of us has a bright window that we can look out and actually at night we can stargaze from our bed which we were really happy about when we found that out. And then over here I have a my own personal book shelf where I have some photographs and some of my personal books and journals and some more of my personal items. And then we've also hung up crystals in our window um because the light shines in here beautifully and it projects rainbows in our room and it's so lovely in here when the light comes in and we get to see rainbows on our walls. So, one of the things that was really important for us since we are in a smaller space with our two teenagers, we wanted to make sure that our bedroom felt like a little bit of a sanctuary and a space that we could close the door and just have some quiet time. So, we did keep it really bright. So, when we need that bit of privacy, we can just come in here and shut the door and have some quiet time. This is my room. It's right above the bathroom and it's 8x 10 ft. I have my bed over here and then my window right above my bed so I can look out and it opens for some air flow in my room. I do cheer and volleyball and I keep most of my volleyball and cheer stuff under my bed right here. And then I have my backpacks behind my bed to keep my stuff for school and my sports backpacks. I have my dresser with all my clothes and stuff and then my mirrors to get ready in the morning and then all my jewelry hanging on the wall. I do have a pocket door to close off my room from noises downstairs and then I can have a space to get ready in the morning by myself. >> This is my room. It's on the opposite end of the tiny house as my sister's room. It's roughly 8x10 ft. One of the design features in my room is it is a little bit taller than my sister's room cuz I am taller. That means so my parents bedroom is a little shorter, but they're both short, so it's okay. My room also has a second window just because it was going to go above the door like due to structural integrity, it could not be there, so I got it. My bookshelf is also on the left. It's I'm really into World War II, so it's full of World War II books and novels. Uh, and then I have some other storage. Also, when I moved into here, I didn't have to get rid of too much um cuz I grew so much. Most of my clothes and all that I'd already grown out of, so it wasn't too hard to get rid of everything that I had in my old room. This is our tiny home. It's 40 ft long and 10 ft wide, so it's fairly large for a tiny house. In order to build that size, we needed a 40ft gooseeneck trailer to hold the weight and the size of it. We travel a bit. We work a lot. We've got kids that demand a lot of our time. And so easy maintenance was really important to us. So the siding, we chose metal for most of it, but we just couldn't help ourselves cuz we really love the look of cedar. So we have the cedar around the front door. And you'll notice on the rest of it, we use a lot of windows to bring in that natural light. We also added a deck onto the front. And we spend a lot of time out here, whether it's cooking, eating with friends. We actually have a moving dining room table that we bring out here. We'll be out here quite a bit when the weather's nice enough, which even in Washington, it's nice a lot of the time. You'll notice that a lot of our design and this house in particular is pretty utilitarian and that's because we're just so busy with the rest of life and owning our own business and our kids and travel which are all important to us. So we one of the things we love about the tiny house is that we don't spend a lot of time on maintenance and cleaning and that allows us to free up our time and our lives to do all those other important things. I think the most impactful part about living in a tiny house, especially with kids. I feel like the world right now is trying to disconnect us from our kids. Like they have phones, they are busy with sports. There's so much distraction and both parents are supposed to work and it's pulling you away from that core family unit. And we've always been close. Like when the kids were young, we're really close, but now that they're becoming teenagers, we've maintained that closeness as a family. And I I really do think a big part of that is that we just spend so much time in the same space and the kids are allowed to be outside and they're able to go express themselves through out the outdoors and we've just raised some really awesome teenagers and I I do think that their lifestyle that they have out here helps with that and as parents too now I'm not spending hours cleaning the house and I get to spend time with them. Another thing that I think is important to both of us is that we really want people to experience what we have experienced. And we happen to build tiny homes, but whether we build one or someone else or you build your own, like this lifestyle is actually very attainable. And it allows a without all of the distractions. And I would just encourage anybody to take that step. Go for it. Give it a try. I mean, it has really been great for us. If you want us to help you, we're happy to do that. But if you want to do it yourself or find someone else, just encourage you to go for it. Thank you so much for coming along this tour today with us in our tiny house. >> Yeah, we really appreciate it. You can find out more information about us and our business at oceanddesigns.com. It's oshndesigns.com. We actually just merged businesses with big freedom tiny homes. So oceansigns and big freedom are all the same business now. You have your solar arrays, you're totally off-grid, which makes this property honestly that much better. So what do you need to call your home an off-grid home? First, you need water. This home has its own water supply weld 500 plus feet into an underground aquifer with a state-of-the-art water filtration system. You'll also own the water rights, meaning you'll never have to pay for a single drop of water. Second, you need power. A lot of power. An average American home uses around 30 kilowatt-hours of power per day. This home has eight solar arrays, each with 15 solar panels. Combined, they produce around 41 kilowatts of power, which is stored in a series of large batteries that can hold up to 300 kilowatt-hours of storage. That battery storage can easily power the whole home for several days. The solar panels are also built to move so during different times of the year, you can change their direction to always get the maximum amount of sun exposure on your solar array. If you somehow manage to need more power than the solar panels and the battery storage can handle, then you have six 1,000-gallon propane tanks that run a generator that can power your whole house for months without needing any other power sources. This is our first time to tour a home like this and it's fascinating to see this kinda take on the residential build and how feasible off-grid living is with the new solar tech. And that's it for the backyard. The fact that this home is fully off-grid and self-sustaining makes it so unique and it just offers such a different lifestyle so I'm really glad we came to Utah to tour this incredible estate. - How to Master Networking in 9 Minutes: Kind: captions Language: en Networking is one of the most important aspects of business because deals, jobs, and partnerships don't just go to the most qualified, they go to the most connected. The truth is 90% of people don't know how to network. Most people think networking is about collecting business cards and meeting people. And that's an element of it. The hard part is building relationships and maintaining relationships. And now I'm going to share seven strategies and principles anyone can apply to build their network. Number one, invite everybody. This past year, I was going to a Polar Plunge. I had 20 tickets and I would call people up and invite them, be like, "I'd love to do this event with you. It's a fundraiser. You know, we're going to jump in a frozen lake. It's a Polar Plunge." Now, some would say no, but they still appreciate it and thank me so much for thinking of them. And others would say yes, and then we had an incredible experience and memory together. But the bottom line is I didn't invite 10 of the 20 tickets. Everybody gets an invite. Go out of your way. Invite everybody. Fill up the tables because people feel heard and wanted and they remember and appreciate the ask. Number two, gifts are a powerful weapon. So, I'm a big believer in gifting. Every time that I do a podcast, I send the person that hosted me on their podcast a gift. It should be the opposite. Usually, like the person that does the podcast, maybe they would get a thank you note. I flip the script on that. And I'll say this. It's the gesture and the thought when people least expect it. That's the key. Gift them. Number three, three minute a day rule. So networking isn't about collecting business cards. It's not even about how many people you know. It's about who knows you. And everybody is one meeting, one referral, one introduction away from changing the entire trajectory of your life. So I've always been a big believer in planting a lot of seeds in a lot of different directions. And I take about 3 minutes a day to do it. I'll give you an example. That's Moren's rule. So, one thing you could do is just make a list of people in your life that are important to you and big moments in their life. Call them on the anniversary of those days. What works a lot better are what I call sparkers. Um this is when you ask people to think for all the exciting things in their life. And we found that super connectors, those really, you know, they really know how to do it. They get the most cards stand in a specific spot in every networking event. And this is called the social zone. It's right as people exit the bar. So, when people exit the bar, they kind of have their drink and they're like, who do I talk to? And if you are their social savior, you're that person there. That's that sweet spot where you make really high quality conversations. You know what? - 7 Networking Tips Every College Student Should Know - How To Network Efficiently: Kind: captions Language: en let's be real networking probably isn't at the forefront of your mind right now you're just trying to survive your public speaking class without feeling nauseous or trying to finish your essay that's due the next day but picture this the future you walking across the stage at graduation not having to worry about finding a job because you networked with other people and landed a job doesn't that sound nice but now the question is where do you begin hi guys my name is carla and today i'm gonna be breaking down seven simple networking tips you can start doing while you're in college now if the idea of networking is intimidating to you introvert self you're not alone but with a little motivation and dedication you can actually start building your circle of network during your everyday activities like conversing with your professors and even your classmates your professors may have taught quite a few bright students in the past and odds are they may still be in touch with some of their former students sometimes those former students will email their former professors with links to job openings but you actually talking to your professors it keeps your name in the back of their minds when this question arises of needing to find a job and sure today your classmates may be wearing your pajamas to lecture and look like they just got a bed but who knows where they might land after graduation wouldn't it be nice to build a relationship with them now one day they might just work at your dream company and you can say hey remember me from your calculus class the next tip is getting involved on campus there are a number of benefits when it comes to getting involved on campus you'll make friends add another item to your resume and of course build your network there are a ton of ways to get involved on campus like clubs sororities fraternities sports you name it not only will you be able to connect with a number of other students but you'll also be able to connect with speakers advisors and mentors if your campus has a networking fair or a job fair go that's the school's way of literally handing you a plate of full of contacts that you can network with and chances are your campus has a career center full of free resources and you might be thinking do i really need these resources well yes you actually do this next tip involves taking a trip to your campus career center you can find internships or jobs posted by alumni get tips on how to build your online presence participate in mock job interviews which are so helpful by the way and learn about upcoming job fairs even if you aren't actively looking for a job right now it can't hurt to stop by these job fairs shake hands with company recruiters give them your resume have conversations and learn what's going on in their industry tip number four involves developing your online presence if the idea of talking to strangers makes your hands a little sweaty don't worry you can also network from behind your computer you can start by building your online presence through linkedin now depending on your industry you might also want to make a website or start an online portfolio this is typically for people wanting to go into art interior designing or whatever field that requires a lot of testimonials you can also leverage social media as long as your profiles are pristine and a professional's eye now your school might even have an alumni group on facebook and these can certainly be helpful typically alumni will post job openings to help their fellow undergrads and honestly establishing a networking connection can happen anywhere tip number five includes being open to new connections maybe you're waiting in the line at the cafeteria and you overhear professor talking about something that you are familiar with well then there's your chance to politely chime in or if you're in line at the school coffee shop and you hear a fellow student chatting about a guest lecturer there are always opportunities to network you just have to be willing to put yourself out there and start conversing you never know what could happen and the worst that could happen is that they never talk to you again but the best thing that could happen is that you get a great job opportunity just for striking a conversation and this brings me to tip number six which is don't be afraid to make the first move confidence can go a long way don't treat networking like a game of who can make the most connections or how many resumes can you can pass around instead genuinely take interest in who you're talking to who they are and what they do try to remember important information about them and if you're having trouble keeping a conversation flowing start asking some thoughtful questions like how did you get the position you currently have or what does your day-to-day look like or any advice for a young young person who's looking to get into your industry and last but not least number seven which is maintaining your network i can't emphasize enough that networking is more than collecting business cards passing out resumes and sending requests on linkedin it's hard work and you'll want to invest some time in maintaining your network if you meet someone at a job fair shoot them in emails afterwards saying it was a pleasure meeting them even if they don't have your ideal position available right now let them know you'd appreciate the time they took to talk to you and if you really want them to remember you add in something personal you learn from them while the two of you were conversing this will not only show that you paid attention but that you also learned something valuable from them so those are my seven tips when it comes to networking in college remember to always be yourself and don't be afraid to strike up a conversation at any moment you never know what one conversation can lead to be authentic be humble and things will surely work out for you thanks so much for watching and i'll catch you on the next one
