- How To Hide Real Estate Ownership & Keep Transactions Private: Kind: captions Language: en hi this is the business guy let's talk about transferring real estate privately so it doesn't appear on the public records this means nobody knows about the transfer but you in fact there are two main ways to hide property title transfers and they'll tell you my favorite and what's worked best for thousands of our clients so first we're going to talk about the different types of deeds and what each one does we'll talk about how to hide property transfer from the public and we'll talk about how to save thousands of dollars in transfer tax when you sell or transfer property and if you're watching this on youtube please click the like button below so youtube promotes this video thanks so much and you can also click the subscribe button so that when more videos come out like this you'll get notified okay so if you've purchased property before you likely generally know what a deed is and how it works people use deeds to transfer property to another party to another person to multiple people to companies such as llcs to living trust etc so when you buy property the deed goes to you so what are the different types of deeds they're typically called grant deeds warranty deeds or quit claim deeds what's the difference among these different types of deeds well grant deeds contain two guarantees first the grantor states that the property has not been sold to anybody else secondly it states the property is not burdened by encumbrances apart from those the seller has already disclosed to the buyer that's a grant deed warranty deeds are used all over the united states but are most common in the midwest and eastern states they're very similar to grant deeds with one exception instead of two guarantees like the grant deeds we just covered warranty deeds contain three with a warranty deed next that they will defend and warrant the title against claims of all persons and third the grantor declares that the property is not burdened by any encumbrances apart from those issues about which the seller has already told the buyer in essence the grantor guarantees the grantee that the title is free of any defects that may affect the title even if the defect was caused by a previous owner and finally quit claim deeds are used to convey any interest in the property that the grantor might possess in the property the grantor might be a legal owner or not the deed makes no promise in that regard so in essence a quit claim deed says i'm not saying i have any ownership interest in the property but whatever interest i do have i give to you quit claims are often used in divorce situations to deed a marital property from one spouse to another let's say a married person holds the title to a property and is the sole and separate owner say they acquired the property before marriage the spouse who is not on title might be asked to sign a quit claim deed to ensure they don't later try to lay claim to the property so when we say the word deed here we're referring to one of those three types of deeds that people use to transfer properties from one party to another a deed is typically delivered to the county recorder's office in the county where the property is located the seller signs the deed in front of a notary public then the document is recorded either physically on a piece of paper or in many counties the document is submitted electronically doing so shows the world that you are the legal owner of the property now once the need is recorded there are two ways we will discuss here to transfer ownership privately first you can put the property into a land trust that's our favorite way to transfer property in fact a land trust is a document that we draft that our clients use to own real estate privately we've set up thousands of land trusts over the 30 years i've been in this business our research has shown us that land trusts are legal in all 50 states now some states specifically name land trusts in their statutes some don't but there are also no laws that we know of that say you can or cannot wear red shoes so not everything that we can or cannot do is written in the law book simple common law on which most of our laws are based allows for the use of trusts and has for centuries and we've set up land trusts which have been successfully used in all 50 states in many foreign countries now the cool thing is most of the lenders will only lend to you if you hold the property in your own name however once you get your mortgage the garn saint-germain depository institutions act allows you to put the property into a land trust without the lender calling the loan due the property needs to be between one and four residential units and you're the beneficiary of the trust so step one call us and have us set up a land trust and a transfer deed step two is to have all of the relevant parties sign the land trust and the transfer in front of a notary public step three is to store the land trust in a safe place that you can access you don't typically record the land trust you keep that in a safe place at home step four is to record the transfer deed this creates a public record that the trust slash trustee now holds title of the now we also provide you with an additional important document called the assignment of beneficial interest cutting through the technicalities a beneficiary of a land trust essentially owns the property simply put the beneficiary holds the rights to the benefits of the property that the trust holds so if you are the beneficiary of the trust you can assign your beneficial interest in the trust to someone else such as your llc so that you have protection from lawsuits that originate from that property now your personal residence and your investment properties fall into two separate tax categories we would not assign your beneficial interest in a land trust to an llc if that land trust owns your personal as this could affect tax benefits that you enjoy in your primary dwelling but with rental property we routinely assign beneficial interest to llcs as the tax benefits remain plus you get the tremendous legal protection that the llc can offer someone slips or falls or dies on the property and you have thousand dollars of insurance somebody could sue you for three to four million the llc can protect you from that lawsuit so by assigning your interest in the property to an llc you do that privately with your assignment of beneficial interest in the land trust so now you can show the trust with yourself as the beneficiary and then when a lawsuit comes knocking you can also show your assignment a beneficial interest document this document shows that you assigned your beneficial interest in the trust to an llc so that you have liability protection now it's best practice to have your assignment of beneficial interest in the notarized as third party proof that you assigned it on a certain date in addition to privately transferring the property to a company or another person a land trust can also protect you from paying expensive property transfer fees i just read this morning for example an article about the florida department of revenue a high court ruling enabled the sellers to avoid the documentary stamp taxes by structuring real estate deals in company mergers and foreign land this strategy is often used to conceal the identity of buyers in high-end real estate deals so not only can the land trust help you transfer property privately it can also save you tons of money and transfer taxes by simply assigning the beneficial interest and the trust instead of recording the transfer in the public record so if you want to transfer a three million dollar property to another party in miami-dade county as of this recording the county would charge you eighteen thousand and ten dollars for a single family home and thirty one thousand five hundred and ten dollars in fees for any other type of property such as a duplex or commercial building by using a land trust instead the cost is absolutely zero so the first way to transfer title privately is with a a second way to transfer title privately is with what we call a pocket deed now i don't like doing it this way in most cases but here's how it's done now most people would think in order for the deed to be legally enforceable but you actually have to record it when you notarize a deed to transfer a property you can show evidence of the intent to transfer how do we do that the deed needs to be in writing and preferably notarized so that you have third party proof that you didn't just do it yesterday you'll typically have written on the the tax parcel number i also like to write the street address on the deed so a normal person can read it you can also write or attach a copy of the property legal description in addition to that you also need to have what is called delivery and acceptance that is the one to which the property is being transferred has received a copy of the deed now keep in mind the only one who signs the deed is the person who transferred the property the recipient doesn't need to sign anything in order to receive the property so it'll say for example john smith hereby conveys the following described property to the 123 main street trust with xyz llc as trustee now the manager of xyz llc the property title recipient in this case doesn't sign it just the grantor the one giving the property away or selling the property is the one who signs it so if it was your property before the transfer that would be you so when somebody records the deed it lets the world know that the property is held by the trust slash trustee and not john smith anymore if somebody say the manager of blue sky llc doesn't record the deed then the public records will still show john smith as the owner of the property so john smith could then sell the property to green tree llc say green tree pays for title insurance and it still shows meanwhile john smith moved to costa rica with all of the money from both buyers and then the manager of blue sky llc would have to fight like heck and spend tens of thousands of dollars in court to get the property that's why we have a public recorder's office to prevent this type of shenanigans from happening in fact i had a legal case in the state of washington and in that state the unrecorded deed took precedent i've seen other cases in california which they call a race state meaning whoever races down to the county recorders first wins so that's the second way you can do it and you can see why i like the land trust more than i like the pocket deed but if you live in a state like pennsylvania that charges you out the nose for transferring the property into a land trust and that's the only state i know of that does paying an extra five thousand or ten thousand or fifteen thousand dollars more after the fact and recording the deed that you have notarized five years ago might really make sense and then a few states charge you for transferring the title to property directly into an llc and many don't if you own the property before and you're also the owner of the llc so some terrible lawsuit could strike that originated on the property that could wipe you out and you didn't want to record the transfer deed because you wanted to save the money on the transfer tax delaying paying ten thousand dollars might possibly make sense in a case like that and then record it later because it's a heck of a lot better to pay ten grand then face a lawsuit that could take half a million dollars out of your pocket you'd be happy to pay the 10k and instead of wishing you'd transferred way back when you have a notarized deed that you can record quickly let's say you get into a lawsuit you hurry up and record that pocket deed before the lawsuit freezes the title of the property somebody might ask isn't it a fraudulent conveyance or fraudulent transfer to record the deed after you're sued and the answer is no keep in mind you notarized the deed five years ago you have evidence that the property was transferred five years back so in those scenarios where you're transferring it to an entity you control and you want to avoid the transfer tax you still have an asset protection device that you can deploy when you need it now just keep in mind that you not only have to have had the deed notarized but you also must have kept your llc in good standing throughout the process by paying the annual government and registered agent fees now keep in mind there are over 3006 counties in the u.s they all have their little quirks and we don't know all of them so before doing any of Now, if you're thinking about buying or selling a home in Corpus Christi right now or over the next year, the market is sending two very different signals. Now, on one hand home prices are softening slightly. The average home value sits around $220,000, down roughly about 1%, which could signal a cooling more negotiable market. And that generally gives buyers more room to negotiate here than in hotter Texas markets. But there's another major factor shaping Corpus Christi's outlook, water. And the city remains in a prolonged drought with some reservoir systems dropping into single-digit capacity levels. The region could face a water emergency in a few months, and that matters for housing because Corpus Christi's economy is heavily tied to water intensive petrochemical, energy, and port industries. Now, officials say severe water restrictions could impact industrial operations and future growth, which could eventually affect jobs and housing demand. But what does that mean over the next year? Well, for buyers, softening prices may create opportunity, but they're also entering a market facing long-term infrastructure and water supply questions. And for sellers, homes are still moving, but sellers may have less pricing leverage if inventory continues rising or broader economic uncertainty grows. So, the bigger story here may not just be about interest rates or inventory, but that water risk is increasingly becoming part of the economic conversation around housing. And just like other catastrophic events, drought conditions can have an enormous impact on property value and maintenance expenses. So, homeowners need to be prepared. However if I wanted a nice 2 bedroom apartment I would have to pay $1800,/mo or more. At least I have my own private driveway and windows around all 4 sides of the house. And my neighbors are great. We are all former middle class folks who carry our middle class values with us even as some of our homes are selling as low as $12,000. This is organized theft. It happens every 20 years or so. The long range plan of TPTB is to turn us all into renters like in Europe.

- 5 Rich People's Habits That Will Change Your Life: Kind: captions Language: en I used to think rich people had some secret formula that I didn't know about. Turns out, after studying their habits and implementing them myself, the secret isn't actually a secret at all. It's not about waking up at 4:00 a.m. or taking ice baths. It's about simple, consistent habits that compound over time. So, in this video, I'm revealing five habits that wealthy people practice daily. Habits that I've personally adopted that have completely transformed my relationship with money and success. They aren't the typical save more, spend less advice you've heard a million times. These are the psychological and behavioral shifts that I truly believe separate rich people from everyone else. Number one, they act anyway. When I first started my YouTube channel, my inner voice was brutal. You're not qualified enough. There are people out there who have more years of experience than you. You're not a good talker. Who are you to give advice? That voice kept me from starting for months. And then the same thing happened very recently. I was invited to a big event to speak and the inner critic that comes back regularly came back again. You're probably going to embarrass yourself. You're going to mix up your words and make a fool out of yourself and go blank again. There are people who are in the world who can do it better than you. But every wealthy person I've seen, spoken to, studied, they all have this ability to act despite their inner critics screaming at them. They have this ability to go ahead and do the thing and tell themselves that they deserve it. The difference isn't that they don't have an inner critic. It's that they've learned to recognize it as just noise. And just knowing that, knowing that everyone has this inner critic, but the difference is how you respond to it has changed everything. And now every time my inner critic starts talking, I literally think to myself, "Thanks for sharing." And then I do the thing anyway. Not because I feel confident, but because I've learned that confidence comes after doing the thing, not before. Your inner critic wants to keep you safe, but safe rarely builds wealth. The next time you have an idea for a side business or you want to invest in something new or you want to try a new idea, notice that voice inside you that's telling you all the reasons why it won't work and why you shouldn't start. And once it comes, thank it and move forward anyway. Number two, they realize the importance of obsession. in August 2022. I'd been working on my channel for about 7 months, and it was getting some views, maybe a few hundred, some comments here and there, but nothing really life-changing. And then it was around September 2022 when I became utterly obsessed. And I mean, it was the only thing I thought about in the mornings before work, during my lunch breaks, when I got home in the evenings, on Friday nights when there was a birthday or something else or a social plan, I'd always leave early so I could get a good night's sleep before waking up at 6:00 a.m. on Saturday to work on the channel all weekend. And those few months were probably the loneliest in my life, not in terms of physical proximity, but in terms of people knowing what I was going through and actually understanding it. well-meaning people in my life who wanted the best for me, told me I needed balance, that I was going to regret working so hard, that I needed to remember to have fun and enjoy my life, and they meant it because they cared for me. But you can't have anything big or different by doing the same thing you've always done or without being completely obsessed with the thing that you want to do. To get from zero to one, there needs to be obsession. There's just no other way. to get from one to 10. Yeah, sure you can find the balance. You can bring in other parts of your life, but it's just not possible when you're going from zero to one. You have to take a lot of sacrifices that only a few people are willing to do. Every person I know who's done something extraordinary has had a period where they've become completely obsessed with one thing. Not balanced, not well-rounded, just obsessed. So have seasons of your life where you go all in on building something meaningful. That's where true wealth lies. It's the intersection of something you actually enjoy, something that has the potential to generate significant income, and aligns with your natural strengths. Once you find that sweet spot, go all in. Next, we have they question everything they've been taught about money. Why do we think it's normal to work for 40 years and then retire? Why do we accept that only certain people can build businesses? Why do we think that investing is risky, but having one income source isn't? Most people never question the money beliefs they grew up with. A lot of my beliefs included things like you shouldn't talk about your finances with other people. You have to work in a job that you don't enjoy if you want to make money and live a good life eventually. And things like money doesn't grow on trees and it's hard to make money. These beliefs keep you in a certain way of living even if it's not the life that you want. And it's only when I started questioning everything that I knew that I was then able to attach a different definition of money. One that fitted the life that I wanted to create and what ultimately made me happy. And it was one that led to me quitting my investment banking job, starting this channel where I openly talk about money and my financial situation and found ways to make money online, which when I was working in a traditional job, thought making money online is just a lie. and that anyone who was saying that they're making money online is just lying because I wasn't open-minded enough to realize that there are other ways to create wealth that didn't require you following the traditional path. The money rules you grew up with probably don't fit your current life and you have every right to question them. What if the opposite was true? What if there were different ways to think about money that you've never even considered? The start of a new life for yourself starts with a new mindset. And they make money while they sleep. This one took me the longest time to understand, but it's probably the most important. There is a fundamental difference between how most people think about earning money and how people who have a lot of wealth and have a lot of money think about it. Most of us are taught that earning money requires our time and it requires our physical presence. You show up, you do the work, you get paid. But the people on the other side have figured out something completely different. They've learned how to decouple their time from their income. They find ways to make money even when they're not actively working. When they're sleeping, when they're on holiday, when they're spending time with family. When I was working in banking, every dollar I earned required me to show up, to work for it, to ask for it, to negotiate it. And if I didn't work, I didn't get paid. That's a really limiting way to build wealth because you can never stop. You're essentially trapped in a cycle where your income has a ceiling and there are only so many hours in a day you can work. There are only so many weeks in a year that you can work. There are only so many years you can work for. The richest people build systems, create products or invest their money that generate income without their constant involvement. For me, this involved creating content that could be watched millions of times after I made it once. It meant building products that can be sold automatically online. You have to be finding ways to make money work for you. Whether that's through starting a business or investing, they both have different levels of risk, but both of them supplement your income and ultimately can lead to a way of you making money without you trading your time for it. By the way, if you are interested in creating a path to reach financial freedom, I have a boot camp coming up which will take you through everything you need to know to create that path in six weeks. Even if you feel intimidated by jargon, if you have never invested before and you have no idea where to start, the weight list is currently open and you can join through the link in the description below. And they treat failure as data. I used to be terrified of failing at anything. If a presentation didn't go the way I wanted, I'll think about it and take it personally for weeks. When I failed at different businesses that I tried, I thought, I'm just made for the corporate life through and through. I'm not an entrepreneur. Like most people, I wanted to avoid failure, like it it was going to kill me. But the richest people and the wealthiest people I know, they collect failures like they're collecting data points. Every wealthy person I've studied has failed at multiple things before they succeeded. The difference is how they processed those failures. Instead of saying, "I'm just terrible at this," they think, "What can I learn from this? What can I learn from this?" And then take with me into the next thing that I'm going to try. Instead of thinking I should give up, they think, what would I do differently next time? And I'm starting to do this more and more now. Every time I want to create something and I'm scared about it not working, I just do it. I just like pluck up the courage and I just say, "It's fine. I'm going to work it out. I'll figure out how to do it. And if it doesn't work, I'll learn the lessons for next time." This simple practice has completely changed just how I view setbacks. Failure isn't the opposite of success. It is part of the success. You want to understand this at a deep level. requirements for buildings it basically establishes the standards for maintaining building components like the structure's exterior plumbing mechanical electrical fixtures sanitary installations and firefighting equipment which is why every professional in the property maintenance field should be on top of it and the international property maintenance code is distributed in a book format and it has specifications like the ideal temperature of heated water water heating equipment recommendations and how water should be circulated for example the code is renewed every three years and is available for adoption use by jurisdictions internationally now let's dive into types of property maintenance workers there are three key players involved in the field property managers maintenance administrative duties like advertising vacancies screening tenants coordinating property showings administering contracts managing evictions and disputes and making sure the property is safe activities you can add to your pm program include inspecting plumbing fixtures and systems servicing air conditioners testing smoke and co2 alarms recharging fire extinguishers cleaning window wells and gutters to ensure rain can flow freely and sealing gaps in windows doors and walls to reduce the prevalence of pests number two prioritize maintenance funding when so many other issues in your property demand funding it can be tempting to cut back on maintenance spending but this can lead to rapid asset deterioration in an uncontrollable deferred maintenance list which creates an ideal setting for safety hazards usually it's best to budget a minimum of between two to four percent of your property's current replacement value for your annual maintenance budget every dollar you spend on maintenance should save you at least five dollars in the long run and number three buy and use durable components to keep your property in good shape use replacement parts that operate safely and last as long as possible when you stock your spare parts inventory with high quality goods like fan belts hvac parts light bulbs motors pumps and more you're investing in longevity of your property although very durable items may cost a little more at the onset to help you save on future repairs and minimize frequent rework problems and number four enable regular property audits adopt a custom - This Mom Shares Life Co-Owning a Home With Her Dad | Real Moms Real Money | Parents: Kind: captions Language: en I'm Christa Battaglia. I'm a mom of two, and I co-own a house with my dad. Our decision helped my family not only afford the house of our dreams, but it also strengthened our relationship. I grew up being taught to save as much as you can. My husband and I got married pretty young, and we actually went to high school together. So we had a lot of experience knowing what each other's families were like in terms of money. We were always trying to save money all through our careers prior to having children. I managed to find a job at Northwestern and I've spent most of my career there. But once you have children, everything, money becomes a bigger issue across the board. We were living in a two bedroom condo, and it just was feeling pretty small at that moment. We did know that we wanted to have a second child. Adding a second child into the mix is obviously double the expenses, so we were thinking, "are we going to move into a house?" We couldn't afford much in our neighborhood that we live in. My mother passed away somewhat suddenly, so we had the discussion with my father about joining forces financially and getting a place together. We did a lot of legwork to try to figure out the structure of how we actually owned the building together, and we spoke with a financial planner and also with a tax attorney. It did take a little while to convince my dad. He was really attached to his house where he had lived with my mom for 46 years, and the thought of leaving that was just really hard. But I think what really changed it was one Halloween, we had him come out with us in our neighborhood to go trick or treating, and he kind of saw like, "Oh, I can have some experiences with my grandkids like this." We agreed that we were going to start looking for a place and we actually looked for a couple of years. During that time, my daughter was born and the need just became more and more pressing. We were very certain that we were definitely going to get a place with two units so that you're not necessarily sharing all of the common spaces. When we found this place, we knew it was right, because it had a duplex on top of an apartment. It has the location that we wanted, and price point was really nice on it. We agreed with my father that he would pay half and we would pay half. He took all of the money from the sale of his house and my husband and I took out a 30-year mortgage for our half. We officially became co-owners in the Fall of 2015. It took about a year to renovate the place before we actually moved in. We needed to renovate the top floor to make it into three bedrooms. We did actually have extra soundproofing put in, in the place where we knew we'd be spending the most time. The utilities were set up so that my dad has his own electricity and we have our own electricity. When we bought the place, everything seemed fine. But when we took possession of it, we smelled gas and this meant that all the gas lines were going to be redone as well. When they started tearing open walls, they realized, "Oh, certain support beams weren't actually attached." So there were some surprise expenses. We used a lot of the money that we had saved over the years, and then we added that to what we got from the condo to pay for our portion of the renovation, so, it was a gamble. We talked to a financial planner about it, and it may be kind of risky now, but what is the payoff? And the payoff is now we own a property. And if we ever moved and sold this place, we should be able to make that money back. So it seems like a good financial risk to take. When you buy a place with a family member, you need to talk about money. We don't necessarily like share all of our financial information with my father, but the way that we work it out is that he pays for the homeowners insurance and the water and garbage, and we have to pay our property taxes in escrow. So we pay two thirds and my father pays one-third. I think the biggest impact of owning the home together is that it makes my husband and I more financially secure. If we had bought a single family home in this area, we wouldn't be putting the money into the college fund or into saving for our own retirement. So I feel like my dad agreeing to do this is really a gift that keeps on giving. My kids love living with grandpa. They love dropping down there and being able to socialize on a moment's notice. I also think that it gives my kids a new outlook on money, and I hope that it teaches them that there are different ways to create that flexibility in your life. You don't have to do things exactly the way you see everyone else doing them. The funny part about all of this is that what's old is new. People used to live this way a lot, and now we're just rediscovering it. It gives my children a connection to what used to be in the sense that we don't just all our separate ways. And it really opens up your options for how you're going to live the rest of your life.

- She Left Mumbai to Start Her Own Farm? | Life Beyond Freedom: Kind: captions Language: en Ananda is located on the outskirts of panula which is part of the chandigar Tri City we live here with our uh four adopted dogs 40 species of birds in a forest that we've built which has over 5 or 6,000 trees we grow about 150 crops we are fully self-sufficient in food so when we bought the land here in 2010 it was about 25 to 30 lakhs an acre after covid the land prices have shot up quite a bit and they might be even 10 times of what they were back [Music] then so my life here on the farm is a slow life relative to the bustling City life even though we had bought this land in 2010 we used to still live in Mumbai and we would travel here we would do the plantations we would you know design the Landscapes do the rainwater harvesting but we would go back to Mumbai because that's where we lived and this started influencing a lot of our habits back in the city as well I've spent almost my entire life in Mumbai my first job was with Unilever when I started my career in 1997 my salary was 20,000 rupees a month uh it was a good salary and within a few months I was going to get married so that we don't have to constantly think about money I think Financial Freedom could probably have you know three stages the first stage is when you can earn what you think you want to earn or what you comfortable about that I think maybe our parents generation still comes from that mindset that you know if you're getting more why don't you take it if if you can earn for 20 more years why will you stop earning today so what if you have much more money and many more houses and things like that but I think um we we don't have the same kind of perspective so for example I think the time that we've spent with our daughters before they've left for college is far more valuable than another piece of property or another mutual fund deposit or some something else somewhere else and it's just a question about what you value in life and what you don't when people make a very sudden move to the countryside what happens is that they don't understand the countryside and they tend to carry their urban lifestyle into the village so they'll buy a piece of land the first thing they want to do is build a house centrally air conditioned 10,000 square ft swimming pool this that it's at complete odds with the village so what have you done you've actually done a disservice to the environment and to the people here so when you decide to come here you have to adopt the lifestyle also and not just the benefits you also have to integrate into the community you have to speak in the local language you have to attend the local weddings the local funerals you have to get involved in their lives Suk you have to be part of that you can't uh lead a isolated City rich person life in a poor Village you have to really integrate with them when we decided to finally start building the house and moving into Ananda as our primary and permanent residence we knew that we couldn't build a conventional cement and steel house because that would be an insult to the ecosystem that we had built so our house is made of ramed Earth stone and lime when we were building this house we wanted to build something that would last at least 100 years so that we are not burdening our children with the expense and the effort of renovating this house constantly building a house like this costs as much much as a normal house would in terms of absolute amounts but the difference is that in a normal modern house you probably spend 70% of the budget on materials and 30% on labor but in a house like ours we've spent 70% of our budget on labor and 30% on material and you know we are happy that we've put money into people's pockets instead of blowing it up on materials it took 20 months to build and I know that during that time many of the people who worked on our house earned enough money to build a house of their own in the village and so for us this is a way for us to sort of transfer the wealth back into the countryside back to the poorer sections back to uh smaller towns and boost that economy I think City life is great while you're earning and going to an office if you're not earning in the city it is my personal belief that you should not be living there we need to make way for other people to live that will help reduce the overcrowding that we are seeing in these cities in fact instead of doing this I find that a lot of people are bringing their parents from small towns to big cities even when we used to live in B Bombay like many times we would uh invite our parents to come see us and my father I remember used to feel extremely claustrophobic in my house he used to sit in the balcony all the time and that time I used to feel a little offended you know that what is this you're coming to my house and feeling claustrophobic but now I understand what that feels like we feel we have the most luxurious lifestyle we feel this is real wealth being surrounded by Clean Air ask people who live in GGO who live in Delhi who live in Noida what is clean air and how much value that has you know ask people who are now eating contaminated food and suffering from cancer that what is the value of clean food these are invaluable things again the way we socialize with people has changed in Mumbai we would meet larger groups of people over brightly lit evenings now we meet smaller groups of people in dimly lit evenings in fact we've probably had more friends visiting us here in the last 2 years than in the last 20 years so it's been really really wonderful and I doubt if I was living in the city that we would have had so many visitors who would come and spend you know 2 3 days with us uh at a stretch so when we bought this land it was a Barren degraded piece of land so in 2010 we actually learned about permaculture from a friend and when we heard about permaculture that's when we thought that this was something we could do permaculture stands for permanent agriculture which means how can we create permanent human settlements which are more stable which don't degrade the environment that they live in and which will give us all the resources that we need for ourselves but at the same time not ravage the environment around us so how can we take without depleting we grow about 120 to 150 food crops starting from cereals like wheat and millets we grow all pulses All dlls We Grow probably about 50 60 vegetables across winter and summer we grow all our own spices we also grow oil seeds like peanuts Sesame and sunflower so we have our own source of oil so our philosophy is we grow what we eat and we eat what we grow if we are not able to grow something we assume that it is not the right food for our bodies you know till the time that we were working I think we were so busy working we had no time to manage our investments 2016 my husband stopped working so he had more time on his hands and then he could really start looking at our own financial planning a little bit better and I think he's arrived at the best sort of portfolio mix which gives us both um long-term gains as well as short-term liquidity 40% of our savings would be in real estate 35% of our um wealth would be in equity mutual funds 15% would be in debt mutual fund and about 10% would be in Gold so the 40% which is in real estate also includes Ananda and that is giving us a return on a daily basis as well because we use this land to produce our own food and uh it's our home for everybody who thinks that they are going to buy a piece of land in the countryside start growing food and then sell that food and make some money let me share my experience what I have realized is that there is no money in selling food and the reason being that that we live in a poor country where food will always be cheap so what I recommend to people is to always diversify their revenue streams when they take on a project like this and by all means keep your surplus food as one of the streams of income but not the only one so what I've tried to do here at Ananda is actually I have 7 eight streams of income I'm a seed saver so I have a seed bank of my own and I actually run an online seed shop so that's one of my sources of income people come for permaculture tours that's my second source of income I run sustainable House tours I run Farm stays here I do online consultations for people who are designing their own permaculture Farms or building their own sustainable homes I uh run permaculture courses online and I run a YouTube channel which also gets me money from advertisers so that's my seventh uh source of income and then of course I sell my food so I actually have eight buckets of Revenue and that is what helps me to get enough money to run this farm and to make this whole project self-sufficient so Ananda has been built over 10 years with a lot of effort from not just me my husband and our children but also a lot of the villagers who joined this project and became part of our Farm staff so our children have actually worked on this land from the time that they were 7 and 10 so they have grown up with Ananda and they have a very strong sense of bonding with this place which is different from projects that people take on once their children have left home I meet a lot of lot of people who say this is my retirement plan I'm going to do it when I retire but what happens then is that the children are no longer part of it and when you die the kids have only looked at it as a piece of land that was bought by their parents and they can only think of selling it because they have no emotional attachment to it so I strongly encourage people to start a project like this in parallel to your day jobs firstly that time you're earning incomes so you don't worry about expenses that much you know you can afford things at retirement you only have a passive income you don't want to take on large scale projects that time because you might dip into your savings if today I had to walk into the land that I had walked into in 2010 I don't think I would have had the energy to do the same work so a lot of times when people do financial planning they keep in mind that they need to leave a inheritance for their children I mean I hear this all the time you know or you are buying jewelry or you're buying you know things to give to your children what we forget is if our children cannot breing clean air if they can't drink fresh water if they don't have unpolluted food to eat what are they going to do with all this money you know one of the things my kids tell us mommy Papa we know you did your best okay so we know the world's going to be tough for us but we know that you've done your best so you don't feel guilty about anything and that's a big thing thank you for watching and supporting our Channel if you like the stories we tell here's your chance to be a part of it if you or anybody you know has an interesting Financial journey to share then please fill the form in the pin comment below and we'll get in touch - Psychology Of Luxury | 5-Star Hotel Tricks For Your Home: Kind: captions Language: en imagine waking up in a space that feels like a luxury Escape crisp sheets soft lighting a Serene atmosphere where everything is intentional no clutter no stress just pure Elegance now what if I told you that turning your home into a five-star experience isn't about spending thousands but about knowing a few secrets that even high-end hotels don't want you to know I've spent nights in some of the world's most luxurious hotels from hidden Boutique gems in Paris to high-rise suets in Dubai and over time I started noticing patterns small details that made a huge difference in the way these spaces felt so today I'm breaking it all down how to bring hotel luxury into your home without breaking the bank let's start with the centerpiece the bed ever wonder why Hotel Beds feel so perfect you could be staying at a mid-range Marriott and yet the bed feels like a cloud meanwhile you get home and your bed feels ordinary the secret it's not just the mattress let's reveal their top three secrets on luxurious bedding triple sheeting most people think thread count is what makes luxury sheets wrong hotels use triple sheeting a fitted sheet a flat sheet then a duvet cover and another flat sheet this creates that crisp cool feel that never traps too much heat pillow trick hotels use a mix of down and down alternative pillows so you get a combination of softness and structure if your pillows are lifeless they're the reason your bed doesn't feel luxurious the duvet hack double up on duvet inside one cover for an ultra plush feel did you know most Hotel mattresses are not as high-end as they claim they're often medium firm mass-produced and designed for longevity not Comfort the real magic is in the bedding layers so before you go splurging on a new mattress upgrade your bedding setup first let's talk about lighting the unspoken hero of luxury walk into any five-star hotel and notice this you'll never find a single harsh ceiling light glaring down at you hotels use three layers of lighting ambient this is the soft warm light from sconces floor lamps or even LED strips behind Furniture task lighting think bedside lamps with warm bulbs for evening reading accent lighting subtle highlights like under cabinet lighting in kitchens or candles in the bathroom what's the mistake most people make relying only on overhead lights we have a video where we talked about these three types of lighting for small spaces I will leave the link in the description below I once stayed at a resort in Kyoto Japan and the room was a literal Le in lighting the moment I turned on the lights it wasn't just bright it was cinematic soft glows shadows in the right places it was like stepping into a dream and guess what the room wasn't even that fancy it was just lit perfectly the fix swap cold LED bulbs for warm tone bulbs add a dimmer and layer lighting boom instant luxury let's talk about scent the invisible luxury luxury hotels have a signature scent walk into a Ritz Carlton or a Four Seasons and the air is different it's subtle it's fresh it's relaxing here's how they do it and how you can copy it essential oil diffusers unlike cheap air fresheners these create a soft lasting scent try bergamont plus vanilla for that Spa vibe scented linen spray a hotel trick lightly Mist your sheets with lavender spray before bed hidden candles instead of lighting them place them in your closet or drawers to keep clothes lightly scented we get used to the smells of our home so we don't notice them but guests do that's why hotels refresh their scent constantly avoid clutter the enemy of luxury this is where minimalist meets high-end design luxury is not about owning less it's about curating better hotels never have piles of random junk too many decorative items competing for attention overstuffed Furniture instead they focus on intentional design I once stayed in a hotel in Santorini that had almost nothing in the room no TV no desk just a bed a chair and a view and it felt richer than any luxury Suite I'd been in why because everything had purpose if you want your home to feel high-end start removing things not adding here's where we take it next level use big hotel style towels oversized white plush small towels equal cheap large highquality towels equal luxury let's see the secret final touches that make a room feel expensive trays and layering hotels love using trays for perfumes coffee setups or bathroom essentials mirrors and reflective surfaces these make small spaces feel bigger and brighter custom artwork instead of generic wall art frame personal photos in black and white instant high-end touch all right so now you know exactly how to
